title: Composables & Recomposition
summary: A @Composable function describes UI for the current state. When the state it reads changes, Compose re-runs only that function (recomposition) to update what's on screen — so your code declares "what" the UI should be, never the step-by-step "how" to mutate it.
explanation_html:
Jetpack Compose is a declarative UI toolkit. Instead of building a tree of widgets once and then imperatively mutating them (textView.setText(...)), you write @Composable functions that describe what the UI should look like for the current data. The mental model is a single equation: UI = f(state). Your composable is f; you feed it state; it emits UI. You never hold references to the views and poke at them — when the data changes, you re-run f and Compose figures out the screen difference for you.

A @Composable function is an ordinary Kotlin function annotated with @Composable. That annotation isn't cosmetic: it changes the function's calling convention. The Compose compiler plugin rewrites every composable to receive a hidden Composer parameter, which is the runtime's connection to the in-memory tree (the slot table) where Compose records what you emitted and which state you read. Because of this, composables can only be called from other composables — that's why setContent { } exists as the bridge from the regular world into the composition. Composables return Unit; they emit UI into the tree rather than returning a view object.

Recomposition is the act of re-running a composable when an input it reads has changed, to keep the UI in sync. The magic ingredient is Compose's snapshot state system: when you read a State<T> (typically via mutableStateOf + remember) inside a composable, the runtime records a subscription. When that state's value changes, Compose invalidates exactly the composables that read it and schedules them to re-run on the next frame. Crucially, recomposition is scoped and skippable: Compose tries to re-execute only the smallest enclosing function whose inputs changed, and it skips any composable whose parameters are unchanged and stable. So a counter button tapping count++ re-runs the little Text showing the number — not your whole screen.

This is why composables must follow strict rules. They should be idempotent and side-effect-free: calling one with the same inputs must always produce the same UI, because Compose may run it often, in any order, in parallel, or skip it entirely. Never mutate a global variable, write to a file, or update a var from inside the composable body — that work belongs in event callbacks or dedicated side-effect APIs like LaunchedEffect. They're also named like nouns, PascalCase (UserProfile, SettingsCard) precisely because each one is a piece of UI, not an action you perform. And you must not rely on execution order or how many times a composable runs — treating the count of recompositions as meaningful is a classic source of bugs.

Two consequences fall out of this model. First, where you put state matters. State read inside a composable drives that composable's recomposition; state declared with plain val/var won't trigger anything and will be reset on every recomposition unless wrapped in remember. Second, you'll want to hoist state: instead of a composable owning its own state, lift the value up to the caller and pass it down as a parameter plus an onValueChange callback back up. A composable that takes state in and sends events out (no internal state) is called stateless — it's reusable, testable, and previewable. This pattern, value: T down / onChange: (T) -> Unit up, is the backbone of nearly every well-built Compose UI, and it's the natural extension of UI = f(state): make the state an explicit input so the function stays pure.
key_points: A @Composable function describes UI for the current state; it returns Unit and emits into the tree rather than returning a view object.
The model is declarative: UI = f(state). You re-run the function with new state instead of mutating views.
Recomposition = re-running a composable when state it READ changes. It is scoped (smallest affected function) and skippable (unchanged stable params are not re-run).
Reading a snapshot State<T> inside a composable subscribes that composable to it; changing the value invalidates and schedules recomposition.
Composables must be idempotent and side-effect-free — they can run often, out of order, in parallel, or be skipped, so the body must only describe UI.
Composables are named like nouns in PascalCase (UserProfile, MessageRow) because each call site is a piece of UI, not an imperative action.
Never assume how many times or in what order a composable runs; do side effects in callbacks or effect APIs like LaunchedEffect, not in the body.
Plain local variables are recreated on every recomposition; use remember to keep a value, and mutableStateOf to make changes trigger recomposition.
State hoisting (value down, onValueChange up) makes a composable stateless, reusable, and testable — the direct extension of keeping f pure.
gotchas_html:
The misconceptions below trip up almost everyone coming from imperative UI (View, UIKit, HTML/DOM):

  • "It re-runs everything on every change." No. Recomposition is scoped to the composables that actually read the changed state, and Compose skips functions whose parameters are unchanged and stable. The whole screen is not rebuilt each frame.
  • Mutating a plain var to update the UI. Writing var count = 0; count++ does nothing visible — plain variables aren't observed, and they're reset to 0 on the next recomposition. You need var count by remember { mutableStateOf(0) }: mutableStateOf makes the read observable, remember survives recomposition.
  • Doing work in the composable body. Logging, network calls, mutating shared state, or starting timers directly in the body run on every recomposition (which can be many times per frame, in any order). Side effects belong in LaunchedEffect, rememberCoroutineScope, or click callbacks.
  • Assuming order or count of execution. Compose may recompose composables in any order and in parallel, skip them, or run them more often than you expect. Never sequence logic by relying on "this composable runs before that one."
  • Naming composables like functions/verbs. getUserCard() or renderProfile() read wrong — a composable is the UI, so it's a noun: UserCard, Profile. Lint will even warn on a non-PascalCase composable that returns Unit.
  • Forgetting remember. val state = mutableStateOf(0) without remember creates a brand-new state object on every recomposition, so the value never appears to change. remember is what anchors it across recompositions.

playground.intro: Toggle and slide to mutate state, then watch the single composable re-run with new inputs. The label and box don't move themselves — Compose re-invokes f(state) and the result reflects the current values. That's recomposition in action: change a value, the function runs again, the UI matches.

title: State & UDF: remember, hoisting, StateFlow and ViewModel boundaries
summary: Compose state has an owner. Keep local UI element state close to the composable that owns it, hoist shared or screen state to the lowest common owner, expose immutable state plus events, and collect Flow or StateFlow into Compose State at the route boundary.
explanation_html:
Compose gets predictable when every piece of state has one clear owner. A small UI element detail, such as whether a chip is expanded, can live near the composable with remember or rememberSaveable. State that multiple children read or write should move to their lowest common ancestor. Screen UI state that depends on business rules, repositories, permissions, or navigation arguments belongs in a screen-level state holder such as a ViewModel or a plain state holder with the same responsibilities.

The practical rule is state down, events up. Stateless composables receive the data they need as parameters and expose callbacks for user intent: query: String and onQueryChange: (String) -> Unit, selected: Boolean and onSelectedChange: (Boolean) -> Unit. They do not know whether the state came from remember, rememberSaveable, a StateFlow, a repository, or a test. That separation keeps components reusable, previewable, and easy to test.

There are two common shapes. A stateful wrapper owns simple local state and delegates rendering to a stateless content function. A route composable sits at the screen boundary, obtains the screen state holder, converts observable streams into Compose State, and passes plain values plus callbacks into content. The reusable content below the route should not receive a ViewModel; pass only the state and events it needs.

On Android, StateFlow and Flow should be collected with collectAsStateWithLifecycle() so collection follows the UI lifecycle. Platform-agnostic Compose code can use collectAsState(). Other observable types, such as LiveData, must also be converted to Compose State before being read from a composable, because Compose recomposes from observable State reads, not from arbitrary mutable objects.

Keep mutable data out of the UI contract. Expose immutable UI state, such as a data class or sealed state, and expose event methods or lambdas that describe user intent. A ViewModel can keep a private MutableStateFlow, publish a read-only StateFlow, handle events with update, and delegate business work to repositories. Avoid passing mutable lists or mutable data classes as state; Compose cannot observe in-place mutations reliably.

State ownership is not the same as state saving. remember survives recomposition while its call remains in the composition. rememberSaveable saves small, Bundle-friendly UI element state through activity and process recreation. A ViewModel survives configuration changes and owns screen logic, while SavedStateHandle should keep only minimal restoration keys or small UI element state. First decide who owns the truth, then decide what tiny part needs to be saved.
key_points: Every piece of state needs a single owner: local UI element state, shared UI state, or screen state.
Hoist state to the lowest common ancestor that reads and writes it; keep state as close as possible to where it is consumed.
Stateless composables take state parameters and emit events through callbacks; they do not know where the state is stored.
Use a stateful wrapper for simple reusable components and a route composable for screen-level state holders.
Collect Flow or StateFlow with collectAsStateWithLifecycle() in Android UI so collection follows lifecycle state.
Expose immutable UI state from state holders; keep MutableStateFlow, mutableStateOf, and mutation functions private.
Do not pass ViewModel instances down into reusable UI; convert them to plain state and callbacks at the screen boundary.
Avoid mutable lists and mutable data classes as Compose state because in-place mutation is not observable.
Choose saving separately from ownership: remember, rememberSaveable, ViewModel, and SavedStateHandle solve different lifespans.
gotchas_html:
• Passing a ViewModel into every child. The child becomes hard to preview, hard to test, and coupled to a screen scope. Keep the ViewModel at the route boundary and pass values plus callbacks down.
  • Collecting Flow with collectAsState() in Android UI by habit. Prefer collectAsStateWithLifecycle() for Android apps so collection stops and starts with lifecycle state.
  • Two sources of truth. A TextField that keeps local text while the ViewModel keeps another copy drifts during validation, restoration, or submit. Pick one owner.
  • Mutating lists in place. items.add(...) on a mutable list may not notify Compose. Publish a new immutable list value from observable state.
  • Hoisting everything globally. Hoisting improves control only when another owner needs the state. Simple private expansion or animation state can remain local.
  • Saving the whole screen state. Saved instance state is for small restoration data, not loaded collections, bitmaps, or domain models.
  • Event callbacks that expose implementation details. Prefer onSearch(query) or onArticleSelected(id) over callbacks that leak repository, navigation, or mutable state internals.

playground.intro: Use the TextField playground as a controlled state model. The field value is the state flowing down, and every edit calls an event callback flowing up. In production, a route composable often connects that callback to a ViewModel or plain state holder.

title: Modifiers: the chain
summary: A Modifier is an ordered, immutable chain that decorates a composable — padding, background, size, click handling, and more. Each call wraps the previous one, and because layout modifiers are resolved outside-in, the order you write them is the behavior you get.
explanation_html:
A Modifier is how you tell a composable everything about itself that isn't its content: how big it is, how much space surrounds it, what it's drawn on top of, whether it's clickable, scrollable, clipped, and so on. Every standard composable accepts a modifier: Modifier parameter, and by strong convention it is the first optional parameter so callers can pass it positionally-after-required or by name. You build one by starting from the Modifier companion object and chaining: Modifier.padding(16.dp).background(Color.Red). Each function in the chain returns a new Modifier — the chain is immutable, so you never mutate an existing modifier, you only produce a longer one.

The single most important mental model is that a modifier chain is ordered, and order is semantics, not style. Internally the chain is a linked list of Modifier.Elements, and a.then(b) (which is what the dot-chaining sugar compiles to) concatenates them left-to-right. When Compose lays out a node, it processes layout-affecting modifiers from the outside in — the leftmost (first) modifier is the outermost wrapper, and each subsequent modifier operates on the space the previous one handed down. So in Modifier.padding(16.dp).background(Color.Red), the padding is applied first and shrinks the available box; the background then fills only that already-shrunk inner region, leaving a transparent gutter. Flip it to Modifier.background(Color.Red).padding(16.dp) and the background fills the full outer box, then padding insets the content within the colored area. Same two calls, completely different picture.

To read a chain fluently, walk it top-to-bottom and ask "what region does each call receive?" Think of it as nested boxes: the first modifier is the biggest box, and every following modifier draws or measures inside whatever the previous one produced. Size modifiers behave the same way — Modifier.size(100.dp).padding(10.dp) declares a 100dp box and then reserves 10dp of padding inside it, so the content area is 80dp; whereas Modifier.padding(10.dp).size(100.dp) puts 10dp around a 100dp box, giving a 120dp footprint. Drawing modifiers like background, border, and clip respect the bounds established by the layout modifiers above them, which is why a border placed before a padding hugs the outer edge while a border after the padding hugs the inset content.

Several built-in modifiers carry parameters and defaults worth memorizing. padding has four overloads: padding(all), padding(horizontal, vertical), padding(start, top, end, bottom) (each defaulting to 0.dp), and padding(PaddingValues). All distances are Dp — density-independent pixels created with the .dp extension — so they scale across screen densities; padding is also direction-aware, meaning start/end flip in right-to-left locales while left/right do not. background(color, shape = RectangleShape) fills with a solid color (there's also a Brush overload for gradients) and an optional shape that clips the fill. border(width, color, shape = RectangleShape) strokes an outline; the stroke is drawn centered on the bounds. clip(shape) hard-clips drawing and touch to a shape — essential for rounded corners on images, and notably background and border with a shape only round the paint, not child content, so you still need clip to actually crop what's inside.

A few structural facts round out the picture. Because modifiers are plain immutable values, you can hoist a chain into a val and reuse it, or build it conditionally with .then(if (selected) Modifier.border(...) else Modifier) — passing the bare Modifier companion as a no-op is the idiomatic "empty" chain. Order also matters for input: clickable placed before padding makes the padding part of the touch target, while after padding it doesn't. And modifiers always apply to the composable they're attached to, never to its children — a Modifier on a Column sizes and decorates the column itself, and to affect children you either pass each child its own modifier or use scope-specific modifiers like Modifier.weight() and Modifier.align() that only exist inside RowScope/ColumnScope/BoxScope.
key_points: A Modifier is an ordered, immutable linked list of elements; every chain call returns a new Modifier and never mutates the old one.
Dot-chaining is sugar for then(): Modifier.a().b() == Modifier.then(a).then(b), concatenated left-to-right.
Layout modifiers resolve outside-in: the FIRST (leftmost) modifier is the outermost box, and each later call works inside the region the previous one produced.
padding before background = transparent gutter around the fill; background before padding = full fill with inset content. Order is semantics, not style.
padding has overloads: padding(all), padding(horizontal, vertical), padding(start, top, end, bottom) each defaulting to 0.dp, and padding(PaddingValues).
All sizes are Dp (density-independent, via the .dp extension); start/end padding is RTL-aware while left/right is not.
background(color, shape) and border(width, color, shape) only round their own paint — use clip(shape) to actually crop child content and touch.
modifier is conventionally the first optional parameter of a composable, and Modifier applies to that composable only, never to its children.
The bare Modifier companion is a valid no-op chain, useful for conditional building with .then(if (cond) ... else Modifier).
gotchas_html:
The classic traps with modifier chains:

  • Assuming order doesn't matter. padding().background() and background().padding() produce visibly different results. If your background has an unexpected transparent margin, you put padding before it.
  • Expecting background's shape to clip children. background(color, RoundedCornerShape(12.dp)) rounds the color fill, but an image or child drawn on top still has square corners. You need an explicit clip(shape) earlier in the chain to crop content.
  • Putting clickable after padding and wondering why the padding isn't tappable. The ripple and touch target only cover what's inside the clickable call, so place it before padding to make the inset area part of the hit area.
  • Calling .padding() twice and expecting them to cancel or override. They accumulate — each padding insets again, so two 8dp paddings give 16dp total.
  • Thinking a modifier on a layout affects its children. Modifier.padding on a Column pads the column's own edges, not the gap between children; for inter-child spacing use Arrangement.spacedBy.
  • Reaching for scope modifiers in the wrong place. weight, align, and matchParentSize are extensions on RowScope/ColumnScope/BoxScope and won't resolve outside those layouts.
  • Using raw Int instead of Dp. padding(16) doesn't compile (or means the wrong thing); you must write 16.dp.
canonical_code:
import androidx.compose.foundation.background
import androidx.compose.foundation.border
import androidx.compose.foundation.layout.padding
import androidx.compose.foundation.layout.size
import androidx.compose.foundation.shape.RoundedCornerShape
import androidx.compose.material3.Text
import androidx.compose.runtime.Composable
import androidx.compose.ui.Modifier
import androidx.compose.ui.draw.clip
import androidx.compose.ui.graphics.Color
import androidx.compose.ui.unit.dp

@Composable
fun ChainDemo() {
  // Read top-to-bottom, outside-in:
  Text(
    text = "Hello",
    modifier = Modifier
      .size(120.dp)                       // 1. declare a 120dp box (outermost)
      .clip(RoundedCornerShape(16.dp))    // 2. crop everything below to rounded corners
      .background(Color(0xFF6750A4))      // 3. fill that clipped region
      .border(2.dp, Color.White, RoundedCornerShape(16.dp)) // 4. stroke on the edge
      .padding(16.dp)                     // 5. inset the content inside the fill
  )

  // Swap two calls and the meaning changes:
  // .background(...).padding(16.dp)  -> fill is full size, text inset
  // .padding(16.dp).background(...)  -> 16dp transparent gutter around the fill
}

playground.intro: One box, three modifiers. Toggle each on and reorder padding vs. background to watch the chain build outside-in. Notice how moving padding before the background opens a transparent gutter, while moving it after insets the content inside the fill.

title: Modifier Order Matters
summary: A Modifier is an ordered chain applied outside-in, so swapping padding and background changes whether the color fills the outer area or sits inset behind the content.
explanation_html:
A Modifier in Compose is not a bag of unordered style properties like a CSS class. It is an ordered, immutable, singly-linked chain built left-to-right with .then() calls (every helper like .padding() or .background() is sugar for this.then(SomeElement)). Order is the entire point: Modifier.padding(16.dp).background(Color) and Modifier.background(Color).padding(16.dp) produce visibly different results from the same two pieces. To predict the outcome you need a mental model of how the chain is walked, and there are actually two opposite traversal directions happening at once.

Measurement and layout flow outside-in (left-to-right). The leftmost modifier is the outermost wrapper. It receives the incoming constraints from the parent first, gets a chance to shrink or pad them, and hands the reduced constraints inward to the next modifier, and so on until the constraints reach the actual content (the Text, the Image, the children). So in Modifier.padding(16.dp).size(100.dp), padding applies first to the outer constraints and size then constrains the inner region. Drawing also flows outside-in: the leftmost draw modifier paints first (furthest back), then inner modifiers and finally the content paint on top. This is why .background() placement matters so much — background is a draw modifier that fills its own node's bounds, which is whatever region is left after the modifiers to its left have already consumed space.

Now the classic case. With Modifier.background(Color.Coral).padding(16.dp), the background node sits outermost, so it occupies the full size including the padding region; padding is applied after (inside) the background, pushing the content inward. Result: the color fills the whole box and the content floats inset within it — padding behaves like CSS padding. Flip it to Modifier.padding(16.dp).background(Color.Coral): now padding is outermost and reserves a transparent gap on all four sides before the background node even exists; background only fills the smaller inner rectangle. Result: the color is inset and there is a transparent margin around the colored area — padding behaves like CSS margin. The exact same two modifiers, swapped, give you "padding" vs "margin" semantics. Compose has no separate margin concept; you get margin by placing padding before a background/border, and inner padding by placing it after.

The same rule governs every draw-and-clip modifier, not just background. border, clip, clickable, shadow, and graphicsLayer all care about their position in the chain. clip(RoundedCornerShape(...)) only clips what is drawn by modifiers to its right and the content; a background placed before the clip is not clipped by it. clickable defines its touch target as the bounds of its node — padding(16.dp).clickable{} makes only the inner area tappable, while clickable{}.padding(16.dp) makes the full padded area tappable (the padding becomes part of the ripple-able hit region, which is usually what you want for accessible touch targets). size before vs after a background changes whether the background is the fixed size or wraps content. The unifying question is always: "what node bounds does this draw/interaction operate on, given everything to its left has already run?"

A few precise details. All these distances are in dp (density-independent pixels), resolved against screen density at draw time, never raw pixels. The chain is a value: you can hoist a Modifier into a val and reuse it, and Modifier.then(other) concatenates two chains preserving order. Some modifiers are order-insensitive relative to each other because they touch different phases — e.g. two unrelated semantics tweaks — but anything affecting size, drawing, or input is order-sensitive. Finally, repeated padding accumulates: padding(8.dp).padding(8.dp) equals padding(16.dp) of total inset, applied in two layers. When in doubt, read the chain left-to-right as a sequence of nested boxes, each one wrapping the result of everything after it.
key_points: A Modifier is an ordered chain (left = outermost), not an unordered style set; .padding() and .background() are just .then(element) calls.
Constraints and drawing both flow outside-in: the leftmost modifier wraps and paints first (furthest back), content paints last (on top).
background BEFORE padding fills the entire area and insets the content (acts like CSS padding); background AFTER padding leaves a transparent gap (acts like CSS margin).
Each draw modifier (background, border, shadow, clip) fills its own node's bounds — i.e. whatever space remains after the modifiers to its LEFT have consumed theirs.
clip only affects modifiers/content to its RIGHT; a background placed before a clip is not clipped.
clickable hit target = its node bounds: padding-then-clickable shrinks the touch area; clickable-then-padding makes the padding tappable too.
Compose has no margin keyword — you create margin by putting padding before a background/border, and inner padding by putting it after.
All distances are in dp, resolved against density at draw time; consecutive paddings accumulate (8.dp + 8.dp = 16.dp).
Modifier chains are immutable values you can hoist into a val and combine with Modifier.then(other), preserving order.
gotchas_html:
The most common mistakes with modifier order:

  • Thinking order doesn't matter / treating it like CSS classes. Compose modifiers are positional. background(c).padding(p) and padding(p).background(c) are genuinely different layouts, not two ways to write the same thing.
  • Expecting padding to always be "inner padding". Whether padding looks like padding or like margin depends entirely on what draws around it. Before a background it reads as margin; after a background it reads as inner padding.
  • Putting clip() after the background() you wanted to round. background(c).clip(shape) draws the rectangular background first, then clips only the content — the background's corners stay square. Use clip(shape).background(c), or just background(c, shape) which takes a shape parameter directly.
  • Shrinking your tappable area by accident. Modifier.padding(16.dp).clickable{} means only the smaller inner region responds to taps and the ripple stops short of the padding. For a full, accessible touch target put clickable first: Modifier.clickable{}.padding(16.dp).
  • Confusing size position. size(100.dp).background(c) gives a 100dp colored box; background(c).padding(8.dp).size(100.dp) makes the background 100dp + 8dp padding on each side (116dp), because size constrains the inner node, not the outer background.
  • Assuming border and background nest automatically. border(...).background(c) draws the border outside the background's fill region; swapping them draws the background over/under differently. Order them deliberately and add padding between if you want a gap inside the border.

playground.intro: Toggle orderSwap to flip padding and background in the chain. With background first, the color fills the whole box and the content is inset (padding-like). With padding first, a transparent gap surrounds a smaller colored area (margin-like). Adjust the padding and color to see how the same two modifiers produce two layouts.

title: dp, sp & Density
summary: Compose measures layout in dp (density-independent pixels) and text in sp (which also scales with the user's font setting). The framework converts both to physical pixels using the screen's Density, so the same dp renders at a consistent physical size on every device — which is exactly why you never hard-code raw pixels.
explanation_html:
Every size in Compose ultimately becomes physical pixels on a screen, but you almost never specify pixels directly. Instead you describe sizes in dp — density-independent pixels. One dp is defined as the size of one pixel on a 160-dpi ("mdpi") baseline screen. On any other screen the framework multiplies your dp value by that screen's density factor to get real pixels: px = dp × density. So 16.dp is 16 px on an mdpi phone, 32 px on an xhdpi phone (density 2.0), and 48 px on an xxhdpi phone (density 3.0). The visible physical size stays the same — roughly 1 dp ≈ 1/160 inch — which is the entire point: your 48 dp touch target is a comfortable thumb-sized button on a cheap tablet and a flagship phone, with no per-device math from you.

The density value lives in a Density object, which Compose hands you through the LocalDensity composition local. Density carries two numbers: density (the dp→px scale) and fontScale (the user's system font-size multiplier from accessibility settings). It also exposes the conversion helpers you'll occasionally need — 16.dp.toPx(), 50f.toDp(), 16.sp.toPx(), Dp.toSp() — but those extensions only resolve inside a Density receiver scope (e.g. with(LocalDensity.current) { ... }), because the conversion is meaningless without knowing the device's density. In ordinary layout code you rarely call toPx() at all: you pass Dp values straight into modifiers like Modifier.padding() and Modifier.size() and let the framework do the conversion at draw time.

You create Dp values with the .dp extension on Int, Float, or Double — 16.dp, 8.5.dp. Dp is a real type (an inline value class), not just a tagged number, and it supports type-safe arithmetic: 16.dp + 8.dp, padding * 2, (start + end) / 2, and comparisons like if (width > 600.dp). You can coerceIn, take max(8.dp, spacing), and so on. There are also special sentinels: Dp.Hairline (a 0-dp line that still renders one crisp physical pixel, useful for thin dividers/borders) and Dp.Unspecified / Dp.Infinity for "no value" and unbounded cases. Because the unit is encoded in the type, the compiler stops you from accidentally adding a dp to a raw pixel count.

Text uses sp, not dp. An sp (scale-independent pixel) behaves like dp — it accounts for screen density — but it additionally multiplies by the user's fontScale. So fontSize = 16.sp renders larger when a low-vision user bumps their system font size to 130%, while a 16.dp icon next to it stays fixed. This is an accessibility contract: by sizing text in sp you honor the user's stated preference. The flip side is a real layout consequence — at large font scales your text grows but your dp-sized containers don't, so fixed-height rows can clip or truncate. Build text containers to wrap/grow (avoid hard height() on text), and test at 1.3×–2.0× font scale. TextUnit values (sp and em) also support arithmetic, and lineHeight in a TextStyle is typically expressed in sp too.

The cardinal rule: never size UI in raw pixels. Raw Int/Float pixel values mean different physical sizes on different screens — a 48 px button that's finger-friendly on an old phone becomes a tiny 16-dp speck on a high-density display. The only places you legitimately touch pixels are low-level graphics: Canvas/DrawScope work in pixels, some graphicsLayer offsets are in px, and interop with the View system or bitmaps. In every one of those cases you should convert from dp/sp through LocalDensity rather than typing a pixel literal, so your code still respects density and font scale. Compose deliberately makes dp and sp the path of least resistance precisely so you fall into the accessible, device-independent default.
key_points: 1 dp = 1 px on a 160-dpi (mdpi) baseline screen; on any device px = dp × density, so dp keeps a constant *physical* size everywhere.
Create Dp values with the .dp extension on Int/Float/Double (16.dp, 8.5.dp). Dp is a type-safe inline value class, not a raw number.
Dp supports arithmetic and comparison: 16.dp + 8.dp, padding * 2, if (width > 600.dp) — the compiler enforces the unit.
Use sp for text (fontSize, lineHeight): sp scales with density AND the user's system fontScale, honoring accessibility font-size settings.
Never hard-code raw pixels for layout — the same px value is a different physical size on every screen.
LocalDensity.current gives a Density object with density and fontScale, plus toPx()/toDp()/toSp() helpers that only work inside a Density scope.
You rarely call toPx() yourself — pass Dp/TextUnit straight to modifiers and let Compose convert at draw time. Convert manually only for Canvas/graphics/View interop.
Special Dp values: Dp.Hairline (0 dp, still 1 crisp pixel for thin lines), Dp.Unspecified, and Dp.Infinity.
Large font scales grow text but not dp containers, so fixed-height text can clip — let text containers wrap and test at 1.3×–2.0× fontScale.
gotchas_html:
The most common mistakes around units come from forgetting that dp is a physical promise and sp carries an extra user promise:

  • Sizing text in dp instead of sp. fontSize = 16.dp won't even compile (fontSize wants a TextUnit), but the deeper sin is wanting fixed text — that silently ignores the user's accessibility font setting. Always use sp for text.
  • Calling .toPx() outside a Density scope. 16.dp.toPx() fails to resolve on its own; it only exists inside a Density receiver. Wrap it: with(LocalDensity.current) { 16.dp.toPx() }, or read it from a DrawScope/Density you already have.
  • Typing raw pixel literals. Passing a bare Float/Int as if it were pixels (in offsets, graphics, or copied Java code) bakes in a single-device size. Convert from dp via LocalDensity instead of writing pixel constants.
  • Hard-coding heights on text. Modifier.height(48.dp) on a row of text looks fine at 1.0× but clips at 2.0× font scale. Let text wrap/grow; constrain with heightIn/padding, not a fixed height, and test large font scales.
  • Assuming dp == px. It's only true at density 1.0 (mdpi). On a density-3.0 phone 1.dp is 3 px, so reasoning about "pixels" from dp without the density factor is wrong.
  • Reaching for 0.dp to draw a thin line. A true 0 dp border may vanish; use Dp.Hairline when you want the thinnest line that still renders one physical pixel.
  • Mixing units in arithmetic. You can't add a Dp to a TextUnit or to a raw pixel Float — that's a feature. Keep dp with dp and sp with sp, and convert deliberately when you must cross over.

playground.intro: Drag the padding and size sliders (in dp) and watch the live px readout at a chosen device density. The same dp value becomes different physical pixels as density changes — that is exactly what dp protects you from.

title: Sizing: width, height, size & fill
summary: How Compose decides a composable's dimensions: fixed sizes, ranged sizes, fractional fills, required (constraint-ignoring) sizes, and how parent constraints flow down to shape the final result.
explanation_html:
Compose lays out a single pass top-down: each parent hands its child a set of Constraints — a minWidth, maxWidth, minHeight, and maxHeight in pixels. The child must choose a size within those bounds and report it back. Every sizing modifier you write is really a transformation of the incoming constraints before they reach the actual content. This is the single most important mental model for this topic: Modifier.width(), fillMaxWidth(), and friends do not "set a property" on a view — they rewrite the constraints that the next link in the modifier chain receives. Because layout is a single top-down pass (no second measure to resolve circular dependencies), a child can never ask its parent how big it ended up; constraints only flow down, and sizes flow back up.

Exact sizes. Modifier.size(48.dp) sets both width and height; size(width, height) takes two values; width() and height() set one axis and leave the other to wrap its content. These set the preferred size by fixing both the min and max constraint on that axis to the same value. The crucial subtlety: they are still clamped by the incoming constraints. If a parent says "you may be at most 100.dp wide" and you write width(500.dp), you get 100.dp, not 500.dp — the modifier coerces your request into the allowed range. Units are always Dp (density-independent pixels) via the .dp extension; for text-related sizing you'd use .sp, but layout sizing is always Dp.

Ranged sizes. widthIn(min, max), heightIn(min, max), and sizeIn(...) set bounds rather than a fixed value — the content is free to pick any size in that window based on what it measures. defaultMinSize(minWidth, minHeight) is subtly different and very useful: it only applies its minimum if the incoming constraint didn't already specify one. It's how Material components like Button guarantee a 48.dp minimum touch target without overriding a size you explicitly pass. All of these parameters default to Dp.Unspecified, meaning "leave that bound alone."

Fill. fillMaxWidth() makes the composable expand to the parent's maxWidth constraint; fillMaxHeight() to maxHeight; fillMaxSize() to both. Each takes an optional fraction (default 1f), so fillMaxWidth(0.5f) occupies half the available width. Fill is only meaningful when the incoming max constraint is bounded: inside a horizontally scrolling row or an intrinsicWidth measurement the max width is effectively infinite, and fillMaxWidth() there throws or collapses to content because there's nothing finite to fill. wrapContentSize() does the opposite — it lets the content measure itself at its natural size and then aligns that smaller content within the larger constraints, which is how you center a small child inside a fillMaxSize() parent.

Escaping constraints. The requiredWidth()/requiredHeight()/requiredSize() family is the one set that deliberately ignores the incoming constraints. A normal size(500.dp) inside a 100.dp parent yields 100.dp; requiredSize(500.dp) yields a real 500.dp child that overflows (and is centered on) the parent's 100.dp box, clipping or drawing outside as needed. Use it sparingly — for things like an oversized image that must keep its true dimensions. Finally, Modifier.matchParentSize() is special: it exists only inside a BoxScope. Unlike fillMaxSize(), it does not influence the Box's own size — the Box measures itself from its other children first, then the matchParentSize child is stretched to exactly those final dimensions. That's why it's the right tool for a background that should match siblings rather than dictate the size.

Order matters. Because each modifier rewrites constraints for the next, Modifier.width(100.dp).padding(16.dp) means "be 100.dp wide, then inset content by 16 on each side, leaving 68.dp for the content," whereas Modifier.padding(16.dp).width(100.dp) means "make the content 100.dp wide, then add 16 of padding around it, for a 132.dp total footprint." Reading the chain left-to-right as successive constraint transformations is how you predict the outcome every time.
key_points: Sizing modifiers rewrite the parent's incoming Constraints (min/max width/height in px); layout is one top-down pass, so constraints flow down and measured sizes flow up.
size(d) sets both axes; width()/height() set one and let the other wrap content; all take Dp values via .dp.
Fixed sizes are still clamped to the incoming constraints — width(500.dp) inside a max-100.dp parent renders 100.dp.
widthIn/heightIn/sizeIn set min/max bounds; defaultMinSize applies its minimum ONLY when the parent didn't already constrain that axis (used for Button touch targets).
fillMaxWidth/Height/Size expand to the parent's max constraint; the optional fraction (default 1f) gives partial fills like fillMaxWidth(0.5f).
Fill needs a bounded max constraint — using it where max is infinite (e.g. inside horizontal scroll) fails or collapses to content.
wrapContentSize lets content take its natural size and aligns it within larger constraints — the standard way to center a small child in a big parent.
requiredSize/requiredWidth/requiredHeight ignore incoming constraints, so the child can legally overflow (and is centered on) its parent.
matchParentSize only exists in BoxScope and does NOT contribute to the Box's size; it stretches a child to the size the Box derived from its other children — ideal for backgrounds.
gotchas_html:
The recurring confusions on this topic almost all trace back to forgetting that constraints flow strictly top-down:

  • Expecting an oversized fixed size to win. width(1000.dp) inside a narrow parent silently clamps to the parent's max. If you genuinely need the larger size, you want requiredWidth(1000.dp) (and accept the overflow), not width.
  • Calling fillMaxWidth() where there is no finite width. Inside horizontalScroll, a Row with intrinsic measurement, or a LazyRow item, the max-width constraint is infinite. fillMaxWidth() there either crashes ("Asked for infinity") or fills nothing. Give the child an explicit width() instead.
  • Confusing matchParentSize with fillMaxSize. fillMaxSize() reads the Box's max constraint and can therefore force the Box larger; matchParentSize() waits for the Box to size itself from its other children and then matches that — a Box containing only a matchParentSize child collapses to zero.
  • Assuming defaultMinSize always enforces its minimum. It is ignored the moment the incoming constraints already set a min on that axis, so wrapping a fixed-size child in defaultMinSize may do nothing.
  • Ignoring modifier order. size().padding() shrinks the content inside the fixed box; padding().size() grows the total footprint beyond the size. They produce different layouts from the same numbers.
  • Thinking fillMaxWidth(0.5f) means "half of my content." The fraction is of the parent's available width, not of the child's intrinsic size.

playground.intro: Drag width and height to set a fixed size on the child, then flip on fillMaxWidth to see the parent's max width override your width value. Use fillFraction to fill only part of the available width.

title: Modifier.aspectRatio
summary: aspectRatio forces a layout to a fixed width-to-height ratio, deriving whichever dimension is unconstrained from the one the parent does constrain.
explanation_html:
Modifier.aspectRatio(ratio: Float) constrains the element it's applied to so that width ÷ height == ratio. The ratio is a plain Float, not a string like "16:9", and it is always expressed as width over height. So aspectRatio(1f) is a square, aspectRatio(2f) is twice as wide as it is tall, and aspectRatio(16f / 9f) (≈ 1.78) is a classic widescreen box. There is no unit involved — it's a pure ratio applied to the measured pixel dimensions.

The key mental model is that aspectRatio does not set a size by itself. It only describes a relationship between the two dimensions, then leans on the incoming Constraints from the parent to pin down the actual size. The way it works: it tries one dimension, takes whatever the parent allows for it, and computes the other dimension by multiplying or dividing by the ratio. That is why aspectRatio is almost always paired with one fixed dimension — for example Modifier.width(200.dp).aspectRatio(1f) gives a 200×200 square, because the width is fixed and the height is derived as 200 / 1 = 200.

Which dimension does it try first? By default, aspectRatio attempts to satisfy the ratio using the incoming maximum width first, then derives the height from it. It only falls back to other strategies (max height, then the minimum dimensions) if the first attempt produces a size that doesn't fit within the incoming constraints. This default "width-first" behavior is controlled by the second parameter, matchHeightConstraintsFirst: Boolean = false. When you pass matchHeightConstraintsFirst = true, the order flips: it tries to satisfy the height constraints first and derives the width from the height. This matters when the height is the bounded dimension (for example inside a fixed-height row) and the width is what should flex to maintain the ratio.

Concretely, the resolution algorithm tries up to four candidate sizes in order and returns the first one that satisfies the incoming constraints: (1) from maxWidth (or maxHeight first if matchHeightConstraintsFirst), (2) from the other max dimension, (3) from minWidth, (4) from minHeight. A candidate only counts if its computed pair of dimensions is both valid (greater than zero, not infinite) and fits the constraints. If none of the four candidates fit, aspectRatio gives up and the child is measured with the original, unmodified constraints — so the ratio is silently not enforced. This is the single most surprising edge case: an over-constrained context (both width and height tightly fixed by the parent) can make aspectRatio a no-op.

Because aspectRatio derives a dimension from constraints, it needs at least one dimension to be bounded. If neither dimension is finite — for instance an element with no size modifier inside a parent that imposes no max in the relevant axis — there's nothing to derive a number from, and the modifier can't produce a finite size. In practice you guarantee a bounded dimension by adding a sizing modifier (width, height, fillMaxWidth, size, requiredWidth, etc.) or by living inside a parent that already constrains one axis (a fixed-height Row, a fillMaxSize container, etc.). Once one axis is bounded, the other is computed automatically.

Modifier order matters, as always. Modifier.aspectRatio(ratio) sits in the chain and rewrites the constraints passed downward, so a sizing modifier placed before it (closer to the start of the chain) feeds aspectRatio the bounded dimension, and the content/background that comes after it sees the ratio-corrected size. Putting aspectRatio first and then fillMaxSize after it would let the fill override the derived size, defeating the purpose. The idiomatic pattern is set one dimension, then aspectRatio, then decoration.
key_points: The ratio is a Float meaning width ÷ height — aspectRatio(1f) is square, aspectRatio(2f) is twice as wide as tall, aspectRatio(16f/9f) is widescreen.
aspectRatio never sets a size on its own; it derives the missing dimension from incoming Constraints, so pair it with one fixed dimension (e.g. width(200.dp).aspectRatio(1f)).
It needs at least one bounded (finite) dimension to derive from; with neither axis bounded it can't produce a finite size.
Default behavior is width-first: it satisfies the ratio from maxWidth and computes height. matchHeightConstraintsFirst = true flips it to height-first.
Resolution tries up to four candidates in order (max in primary axis, max in other axis, minWidth, minHeight) and returns the first that fits the constraints.
If no candidate size fits the incoming constraints, aspectRatio is silently ignored and the child measures with the original constraints — it becomes a no-op.
The ratio must be a positive, finite Float; aspectRatio(0f) or a negative/NaN ratio throws an IllegalArgumentException.
Modifier order matters: put the sizing modifier before aspectRatio, and decoration/content after it.
gotchas_html:
Common mistakes and misconceptions with aspectRatio:

  • Expecting a size from aspectRatio alone. Box(Modifier.aspectRatio(1f)) with no bounded parent often renders with zero or unexpected size — there's no constraint to derive from. Always give it one fixed dimension or a bounded parent.
  • Thinking the ratio is height:width. It's width ÷ height. For a tall portrait box you want a ratio below 1 (e.g. aspectRatio(0.75f) for 3:4), not above it.
  • Passing a string or "W:H". There is no "16:9" form. Compute the Float yourself: 16f / 9f. And use float division — 16 / 9 in Kotlin is integer division and evaluates to 1.
  • Assuming it always wins. In an over-constrained slot (parent fixes both width and height tightly), aspectRatio silently does nothing because no candidate fits. The box keeps the parent's size and the ratio is ignored.
  • Wrong primary axis. If your bounded dimension is height (e.g. a fixed-height Row) and you leave the default, the width-first attempt may fail or produce surprising results. Pass matchHeightConstraintsFirst = true so it derives width from the bounded height.
  • Order bug: placing aspectRatio before a fillMaxSize/fillMaxWidth lets the later fill modifier overwrite the ratio-derived size. Keep fill/size before aspectRatio, decoration after.
  • Using ratio 0 or negative. The ratio must be a finite, strictly positive Float; otherwise it throws at runtime.

playground.intro: A Box with a fixed width of 200dp. Drag the aspectRatio slider to watch the height get derived as width ÷ ratio: ratio 1.0 is a square, higher ratios make it shorter and wider, lower ratios make it taller. Swap the bounded dimension with fillMaxHeight to see how the resolution flips.

title: Padding — all of it
summary: Padding is the single spacing primitive in Compose — it insets a composable's content within its own box. There is no margin; you control outer spacing with padding on the parent, Spacer, or Arrangement.
explanation_html:
Padding is the one and only spacing primitive in Jetpack Compose, and it is a Modifier. When you write Modifier.padding(16.dp), you are wrapping the composable so that measurement reports a size larger than the content by the padding on each axis, and the content is placed inset from the outer edges. Crucially, padding is consumed from the incoming constraints: the padded child sees a max width/height reduced by the total horizontal/vertical padding before it measures itself. All values are Dp (density-independent pixels) — you must use the .dp extension; there is no implicit unit and negative padding is not supported by the standard padding() overloads (it throws or is coerced — use offset() for negative shifts).

There are four overloads of Modifier.padding, from coarsest to finest: padding(all: Dp) applies the same inset to all four sides; padding(horizontal: Dp, vertical: Dp) sets left/right and top/bottom independently (each defaults to 0.dp); padding(start, top, end, bottom) sets every side individually (each defaults to 0.dp); and padding(paddingValues: PaddingValues) takes a precomputed PaddingValues object. Note the per-side overload uses start/end, not left/right — these are relative to layout direction, so in an RTL locale start becomes the right edge. That is almost always what you want for text and UI that should mirror.

PaddingValues is the data type that represents "padding on four sides" as a value you can pass around, store, or receive. You build it with PaddingValues(all), PaddingValues(horizontal =, vertical =), or PaddingValues(start =, top =, end =, bottom =). It is what scrollable and many Material components accept as their contentPadding parameter. To read individual sides back out you call paddingValues.calculateStartPadding(layoutDirection) and calculateEndPadding(layoutDirection) (these require a LayoutDirection because start/end depend on it), plus calculateTopPadding() and calculateBottomPadding(). The class is also how insets like WindowInsets.systemBars.asPaddingValues() and Scaffold's innerPadding are delivered to you.

If you need padding that ignores RTL mirroring, use Modifier.absolutePadding(left, right, top, bottom). Unlike padding(start, …, end, …), the left and right arguments of absolutePadding are physical edges and do not swap when layout direction is RTL. Reach for it only when a design must be pinned to a physical side regardless of locale (rare) — for everything else, prefer the relative start/end version so your UI mirrors correctly.

The most important mental shift from CSS or XML: Compose has no margin. "Margin" — space outside a component that separates it from siblings — is expressed instead as (1) padding on the parent container, which insets all children equally; (2) a Spacer(Modifier.height(8.dp)) or Spacer(Modifier.width(8.dp)) placed between siblings; or (3) Arrangement.spacedBy(8.dp) on a Row/Column, which distributes a fixed gap between children. Because padding is a modifier, its order in the chain matters: Modifier.padding(16.dp).background(Color.Red) paints the background inside the padding (the inset is transparent), whereas Modifier.background(Color.Red).padding(16.dp) paints the background first and then insets the content, so the padded area is colored. Likewise padding before clickable shrinks the touch target, while clickable before padding makes the padding part of the ripple/click area.

Finally, distinguish padding as a layout inset from contentPadding on scrollables. If you put Modifier.padding(16.dp) on a LazyColumn, that inset clips the scrolling viewport — items scroll under the padded edge and get cut off. Instead, pass contentPadding = PaddingValues(16.dp), which pads the content inside the scroll area: the first item starts 16dp down, the last item can scroll fully into view with 16dp below it, and nothing is clipped. The same idea applies to Row(Modifier.horizontalScroll(...))-style content padding and to component parameters like Button(contentPadding = …), where it controls the inset between the button's edge and its label.
key_points: Padding is a Modifier and all values are Dp — use .dp; there is no default unit and negative values aren't allowed (use offset() for negative shifts).
Four overloads: padding(all), padding(horizontal, vertical), padding(start, top, end, bottom), and padding(PaddingValues). Each per-side arg defaults to 0.dp.
The per-side overload uses start/end (RTL-aware), not left/right — start becomes the right edge in RTL layouts.
PaddingValues is a passable value type; read sides via calculateStartPadding(ld)/calculateEndPadding(ld)/calculateTopPadding()/calculateBottomPadding().
absolutePadding(left, right, top, bottom) uses physical edges and does NOT mirror in RTL — use it only when you must pin to a physical side.
Compose has no margin: use padding on the parent, a Spacer between siblings, or Arrangement.spacedBy for inter-child gaps.
Modifier order matters: padding before background leaves the inset transparent; background before padding colors the inset area.
Padding consumes incoming constraints — a padded child measures against a smaller max width/height than its parent offered.
On scrollables use contentPadding = PaddingValues(...), not Modifier.padding(...), so edge items aren't clipped by the scroll viewport.
gotchas_html:
The mistakes below trip up almost everyone moving from XML, CSS, or Flutter:

  • Looking for margin. It does not exist. Space between two siblings comes from Spacer, Arrangement.spacedBy, or padding on the parent — never a "margin" modifier.
  • Padding a LazyColumn with Modifier.padding. This insets the whole scroll viewport, so the top and bottom items get clipped as they scroll past the padded edge. Use contentPadding = PaddingValues(...) instead.
  • Wrong modifier order around background. background(color).padding(16.dp) colors the inset; padding(16.dp).background(color) leaves a transparent 16dp gap. They look opposite — be deliberate about which you want.
  • Using left/right when you meant start/end. The fine-grained padding() overload only exposes start/top/end/bottom. left/right live on absolutePadding and will silently fail to mirror in RTL.
  • Expecting negative padding to pull content outward. padding() rejects negatives. To overlap or shift beyond bounds, use Modifier.offset(x, y) (which does not affect layout size) — not padding.
  • Stacking two paddings and being surprised they add up. Modifier.padding(8.dp).padding(8.dp) produces 16dp total — modifiers compose, they don't override. The first overload that "wins" is a myth.
  • Calling calculateStartPadding() without a LayoutDirection. Start/end padding is direction-dependent, so the API requires you to pass LocalLayoutDirection.current.

playground.intro: Drag each slider to inset one side of a colored box. Because the background is painted before the padding, the colored area stays fixed while the inner content is pushed inward — so every side's padding is directly visible. Watch how start/end behave as relative edges and how the generated padding(start, top, end, bottom) call updates.

title: Spacing: Spacer & spacedBy
summary: Put gaps between children using a Spacer for one fixed gap, Arrangement.spacedBy(dp) for even gaps between every child, or a weighted Spacer to shove items to opposite ends.
explanation_html:
There are three idiomatic ways to create empty space between children in a Row or Column, and choosing the right one is mostly about how many gaps you need and whether they should be equal. The most literal tool is Spacer: it is an empty, layout-only composable that takes a Modifier and nothing else. You give it a size — Spacer(Modifier.height(16.dp)) in a Column, or Spacer(Modifier.width(16.dp)) in a Row — and it occupies exactly that much space along the layout axis. A Spacer draws nothing (no background unless you add one), so it is purely a gap. Because it is just another child, you have to manually insert one between every pair of items you want separated, which is why a list of N items needs N−1 Spacers.

The cleaner, more declarative tool is Arrangement.spacedBy(dp), passed to the container's arrangement parameter: verticalArrangement for a Column, horizontalArrangement for a Row. It tells the layout to insert that fixed gap between every adjacent pair of children automatically — no manual Spacers, no off-by-one bookkeeping. N children get exactly N−1 gaps; there is no extra space before the first child or after the last. This is almost always the right default for evenly spaced lists and button groups because the spacing lives in one place and stays correct as children are added or removed. It also composes with alignment: Arrangement.spacedBy(8.dp, Alignment.CenterHorizontally) packs the children together with 8.dp gaps and centers the whole packed group within the available space.

It is important to understand that spacedBy is an Arrangement, so it occupies the same slot as Arrangement.SpaceBetween, SpaceAround, and SpaceEvenly — you pick exactly one. The difference is who decides the gap size. With spacedBy(d) you fix the gap at a constant d and any leftover space is left over (handled by the optional alignment). With SpaceBetween/SpaceAround/SpaceEvenly the layout computes the gap by dividing the remaining free space, so the gap grows and shrinks with the container and the number of children. Use spacedBy when you want a designer-specified constant gap; use the Space* arrangements when you want children to spread to fill the container.

The third technique is the weighted Spacer, used to push items to opposite ends. Modifier.weight(1f) makes a child consume all remaining free space along the main axis. A Spacer(Modifier.weight(1f)) is an invisible flexible strut: place it between two items and it absorbs every leftover pixel, shoving the items to the two ends — perfect for a title on the left and an action icon on the right of a top bar. This is the manual equivalent of SpaceBetween for just two groups, but it is more flexible because you control exactly where the elastic gap goes and you can have several weighted Spacers with different weights to distribute space in ratios. Note that weight only exists inside RowScope/ColumnScope, so a weighted Spacer only works as a direct child of a Row or Column.

A few mechanical details. All sizes are in Dp (density-independent pixels) — write 16.dp, and note that 0.dp is the implicit default arrangement (no gap). spacedBy accepts any non-negative dp, and it works in lazy lists too: LazyColumn(verticalArrangement = Arrangement.spacedBy(8.dp)) spaces items without you emitting Spacer items, which is the recommended pattern for scrollable lists. If you need spacing only on the inside but also want breathing room around the whole group, combine arrangement (inner gaps) with a Modifier.padding(...) on the container (outer margin) — the two are independent and do not interfere.
key_points: Spacer is an empty layout-only composable sized by its Modifier — Modifier.height(dp) for a Column gap, Modifier.width(dp) for a Row gap; it draws nothing.
N children separated by manual Spacers need N−1 Spacers; Arrangement.spacedBy(dp) inserts those N−1 gaps automatically.
spacedBy adds the gap only BETWEEN children — never before the first or after the last child.
spacedBy goes in verticalArrangement (Column) or horizontalArrangement (Row); it is one Arrangement and is mutually exclusive with SpaceBetween/SpaceAround/SpaceEvenly.
spacedBy(d) fixes the gap at a constant you choose; SpaceBetween/Around/Evenly compute the gap from leftover space, so those gaps change with container size and child count.
Arrangement.spacedBy(8.dp, Alignment.CenterHorizontally) sets a constant gap AND aligns the whole packed group.
Spacer(Modifier.weight(1f)) absorbs all remaining main-axis space, pushing items to opposite ends; weight only works inside RowScope/ColumnScope.
All spacing values are Dp (e.g. 16.dp); 0.dp is the default no-gap arrangement.
spacedBy works in LazyColumn/LazyRow too, so you never emit Spacer items inside a lazy list.
gotchas_html:
Common mistakes with spacing:

  • Using Modifier.height on a Spacer inside a Row (or width inside a Column). The Spacer only creates a gap along the main axis. In a Row use Spacer(Modifier.width(dp)); in a Column use Spacer(Modifier.height(dp)). The wrong dimension changes the cross-axis size and produces no visible gap.
  • Expecting spacedBy to add padding around the edges. It only inserts gaps between children. If you also want margin before the first / after the last item, add Modifier.padding(...) to the container or use contentPadding on a lazy list.
  • Combining spacedBy with manual Spacers. The gaps add up — a 16.dp spacedBy plus a 16.dp Spacer yields 32.dp. Pick one strategy per axis.
  • Thinking spacedBy and SpaceBetween are interchangeable. spacedBy keeps a constant gap and may leave free space unused; SpaceBetween stretches the gaps to fill the container, so the spacing differs entirely as the container resizes.
  • Calling Modifier.weight outside a Row/Column. weight is defined on RowScope/ColumnScope; a weighted Spacer inside a Box or at the top level won't compile.
  • Emitting Spacer items inside a LazyColumn for spacing. Prefer verticalArrangement = Arrangement.spacedBy(dp) (and contentPadding for edges) so the gaps survive recycling and don't count as extra list items.

playground.intro: Drag the gap slider to set Arrangement.spacedBy, and add or remove children with childCount. Notice the gap appears only between items, never at the ends. Switch arrangement to compare a constant spacedBy gap against SpaceBetween/SpaceEvenly, which compute the gap from leftover space.

title: Offset
summary: Modifier.offset moves a composable visually during the placement phase without changing the space it reserves in its parent — ideal for nudging, overlapping, and animating position cheaply.
explanation_html:
Modifier.offset(x, y) shifts where a composable is drawn relative to where the layout system would otherwise place it, without changing its measured size or the space it occupies in the parent. This is the crucial difference from padding: padding shrinks/reserves real layout space and pushes siblings around, while offset is purely a placement adjustment applied after measurement. The element's slot in the parent stays exactly where it was — only the pixels move. If you offset a child to the right inside a Row, the next sibling does not shift to follow it; the child can even slide on top of its neighbor or out of the parent entirely.

There are two overloads of the dp-based version. The simple one, offset(x: Dp = 0.dp, y: Dp = 0.dp), takes static Dp values with both defaulting to 0.dp. Positive x moves the content toward the layout's end (right in LTR, left in RTL) and positive y moves it down the screen — Compose's y-axis grows downward, so negative y moves up. Because the units are Dp, you must use the .dp extension (e.g. offset(x = 12.dp, y = (-8).dp)); note the parentheses around negative literals so Kotlin parses -8 before .dp.

The second, more powerful overload is the lambda form: offset { IntOffset(x, y) }. It runs inside a Density receiver and returns an IntOffset in raw pixels (Int), not dp. Its real purpose is performance: the lambda is evaluated during the layout/placement phase, not during composition. That means when the offset value changes (for example, driven by an animateIntOffsetAsState or a scroll position), Compose can skip recomposition and even skip re-measurement, re-running only placement. For frequently-changing positions — drag gestures, scroll-linked motion, animations — always prefer the lambda overload over the dp overload, which would otherwise read the changing state during composition and trigger far more work.

absoluteOffset(x, y) and its absoluteOffset { IntOffset(..) } lambda variant behave identically except they ignore layout direction. Regular offset mirrors the horizontal axis under right-to-left (RTL) locales so that positive x always means "toward the end," which is usually what you want for locale-aware UIs. absoluteOffset always treats positive x as "toward the physical right" regardless of RTL — useful when a shift is genuinely tied to screen geometry (e.g. a decorative element) rather than reading order.

Because offset only changes placement, the moved content can be drawn outside its parent's bounds. Whether the overflow is visible depends entirely on whether an ancestor clips: by default most layouts do not clip their children, so offset content can spill over. If you want it cropped to the parent, add Modifier.clipToBounds() (or clip(shape)) on the parent. Also remember offset's effect depends on where it sits in the modifier chain relative to size/background/click modifiers — like all modifiers, order matters, though for a single leaf element offset's visual result usually looks the same regardless of position.
key_points: Offset changes only placement (where pixels are drawn) — it never changes the measured size or the layout space the element reserves, so siblings do not move to make room.
Signature: offset(x: Dp = 0.dp, y: Dp = 0.dp); both args default to 0.dp and are in Dp, so use the .dp extension.
Positive x moves toward the layout end (right in LTR), positive y moves down; y grows downward, so use negative y (e.g. (-8).dp) to move up.
The lambda overload offset { IntOffset(px, py) } takes raw pixels and runs in the layout phase — changing its value skips recomposition and remeasure, only re-placing.
Use the lambda overload for animated, dragged, or scroll-driven offsets; use the dp overload for static design nudges.
absoluteOffset ignores RTL: positive x is always physical-right; plain offset mirrors x in right-to-left locales.
Offset can push content outside its parent's bounds — it is clipped only if an ancestor sets clipToBounds()/clip().
Offset is the right tool to overlap composables, create badges, or fine-tune position — padding is the wrong tool because it reflows the layout.
gotchas_html:
The most common confusion is treating offset like padding. They are fundamentally different:

  • Expecting siblings to move. Offsetting a child inside a Row/Column does not push neighbors. The child keeps its original slot and can visually overlap the next item. If you want real spacing that reflows the layout, use padding or Spacer, not offset.
  • Forgetting .dp or mis-parsing negatives. The static overload takes Dp, so offset(x = 10) won't compile — write offset(x = 10.dp). For negatives, wrap the literal: offset(y = (-8).dp), because -8.dp can read awkwardly and is clearer parenthesized.
  • Confusing the lambda's units. offset { IntOffset(x, y) } returns pixels, not dp. Convert dp to px with the Density receiver: offset { IntOffset(12.dp.roundToPx(), 0) }.
  • Animating with the dp overload. Driving offset(x = animatedDp) reads the animating state during composition and recomposes every frame. Use the lambda overload (offset { IntOffset(..) }) so only placement re-runs.
  • Surprised content gets cut off — or doesn't. Offset can move content out of bounds. By default it is not clipped (many layouts don't clip), so it may overlap unexpected areas; conversely, if you offset into a parent that does clip, it vanishes. Control this explicitly with clipToBounds() on the parent.
  • Wrong tool for RTL. If you hard-code absoluteOffset for a locale-aware nudge, it won't mirror in RTL languages. Use plain offset unless you specifically need screen-physical direction.

playground.intro: Drag the sliders to offset the child inside the faint reference box. Notice the box outline stays put — proof that offset moves the child visually without changing the space it occupies. Try negative values to move left/up, and watch the child spill outside the parent.

title: Column
summary: Column stacks its children vertically, top to bottom. You control vertical distribution with verticalArrangement, horizontal positioning with horizontalAlignment, and let children share leftover vertical space with Modifier.weight.
explanation_html:
Column is the vertical sibling of Row: it lays its children out one above the other, from top to bottom, in the order you declare them. Its signature is Column(modifier: Modifier = Modifier, verticalArrangement: Arrangement.Vertical = Arrangement.Top, horizontalAlignment: Alignment.Horizontal = Alignment.Start, content: @Composable ColumnScope.() -> Unit). The vertical axis is the main axis (the direction children are stacked) and the horizontal axis is the cross axis (the perpendicular direction). Getting this main-vs-cross distinction right is the whole mental model: verticalArrangement controls the main axis, horizontalAlignment controls the cross axis.

verticalArrangement decides how children are positioned and spaced along the vertical (main) axis. The default is Arrangement.Top, which packs everything to the top with no gaps. Arrangement.Bottom packs to the bottom, and Arrangement.Center packs to the middle. The three space values distribute leftover free space between children: SpaceBetween puts the first child flush at the top, the last flush at the bottom, and splits remaining space evenly between them; SpaceAround gives each child equal space on both sides (so edges get half a gap); SpaceEvenly makes every gap — including the two edges — exactly equal. Crucially, these space arrangements only do anything when the Column is taller than the sum of its children; if the children already fill (or overflow) the height, there is no free space to distribute and they behave like Top.

horizontalAlignment positions children along the horizontal (cross) axis. The default is Alignment.Start (left in left-to-right locales, right in RTL), with Alignment.CenterHorizontally and Alignment.End as the other options. Alignment only has a visible effect when the Column is wider than a given child — a child that fills the Column's full width has nowhere to move. A common surprise: a Column wraps its content width by default, so if every child is narrow the Column itself is narrow and alignment appears to do nothing until you give the Column a wider width (e.g. Modifier.fillMaxWidth()).

For per-child control on the cross axis, ColumnScope exposes Modifier.align(Alignment.Horizontal), which overrides the Column-wide horizontalAlignment for just that child. There is also Modifier.weight(weight: Float, fill: Boolean = true), the most important scoped modifier. Weight makes a child consume a proportional share of the remaining vertical space after all non-weighted (intrinsically sized) children are measured. Two children with weight(1f) each take half the leftover height; a weight(2f) child takes twice as much as a weight(1f) child. By default fill = true forces the child to actually occupy its allotted height; pass fill = false to let it be smaller than its share while still reserving that share. Weighted children make verticalArrangement irrelevant, because once weights consume all the free space there is nothing left for the arrangement to distribute.

A clean way to add uniform gaps without an arrangement is Arrangement.spacedBy(8.dp), which inserts a fixed 8dp between adjacent children while still respecting an alignment edge. You can even combine it with an edge, e.g. Arrangement.spacedBy(8.dp, Alignment.CenterVertically). By default a Column sizes itself to wrap its content on both axes; use fillMaxHeight() / fillMaxWidth() / fillMaxSize() or a fixed height/width when you need it to fill the available space so that arrangement and alignment have room to work. Finally, a plain Column does not scroll — if content can exceed the height, add Modifier.verticalScroll(rememberScrollState()) or reach for LazyColumn for long/large lists.
key_points: Column stacks children vertically in declaration order; the vertical axis is the main axis and the horizontal axis is the cross axis.
verticalArrangement controls main-axis distribution (default Arrangement.Top); horizontalAlignment controls cross-axis position (default Alignment.Start).
Space arrangements (SpaceBetween, SpaceAround, SpaceEvenly) only matter when the Column is taller than the sum of its children — otherwise they look like Top.
horizontalAlignment only moves a child if the Column is wider than that child; a fillMaxWidth child has nowhere to go.
Modifier.weight(n) on a child claims a proportional share of leftover vertical space; weights are measured after intrinsically sized children.
weight has fill = true by default (child occupies its full share); fill = false reserves the share but lets the child be smaller.
Weighted children consume all free space, which makes verticalArrangement have no visible effect.
A Column wraps its content by default; use fillMaxHeight/fillMaxWidth or fixed sizes to give arrangement and alignment room to work.
Column does not scroll — use Modifier.verticalScroll for static content or LazyColumn for long lists.
gotchas_html:
The most common Column mistakes nearly all come from confusing the axes or forgetting that distribution needs free space:

  • Swapping arrangement and alignment. People reach for horizontalArrangement on a Column — it does not exist. A Column has verticalArrangement (main axis) and horizontalAlignment (cross axis). Row is the mirror image.
  • "SpaceBetween / Center does nothing." Almost always the Column is wrapping its content height, so there is zero free space to distribute. Add Modifier.fillMaxHeight() (or a fixed height) and the arrangement springs to life.
  • "horizontalAlignment does nothing." The Column is only as wide as its widest child, so narrow children have no room to shift. Make the Column wider with fillMaxWidth(), or the offending child is itself using fillMaxWidth() and can't move.
  • Expecting arrangement to work alongside weight. If any child has Modifier.weight, weighted children eat all the leftover space, so verticalArrangement has nothing left to position and appears ignored.
  • Using Modifier.weight outside ColumnScope/RowScope. weight and align are scope-only extensions; calling them on a top-level Modifier won't compile.
  • Assuming Column scrolls. Overflowing content is simply clipped/forced; a plain Column never scrolls. Use verticalScroll for a handful of items or LazyColumn for large/lazy lists (and never put a verticalScroll Column inside another vertically-scrolling parent without a bounded height).
  • Confusing spacedBy with SpaceEvenly. Arrangement.spacedBy(8.dp) is a fixed gap that does not depend on container height; SpaceEvenly divides variable free space — they produce different layouts.

playground.intro: Stack three children in a Column, then change how they distribute vertically and align horizontally. Notice that the space arrangements only spread the children once the Column is taller than they are, and that horizontal alignment needs spare width to be visible.

title: Row
summary: Row lays children out horizontally left-to-right, controlling the gaps between them with horizontalArrangement (main axis) and their top/bottom alignment with verticalAlignment (cross axis).
explanation_html:
Row is the horizontal sibling of Column: it places its children one after another along the horizontal (main) axis, in the order they are declared in the lambda. Its signature is Row(modifier: Modifier = Modifier, horizontalArrangement: Arrangement.Horizontal = Arrangement.Start, verticalAlignment: Alignment.Vertical = Alignment.Top, content: @Composable RowScope.() -> Unit). The two layout parameters split responsibility cleanly: horizontalArrangement decides how children are positioned and spaced along the row (the main axis), while verticalAlignment decides where each child sits across the row's height (the cross axis). The defaults are Arrangement.Start and Alignment.Top, which is why an untouched Row packs its children flush against the left edge, aligned to the top.

Main axis — horizontalArrangement. A Row measures its children, then uses the arrangement to distribute them within whatever width it has. The position-based values Start, End, and Center push the whole group of children to one side or the middle, leaving any leftover width as empty space on the other side(s). The space-distributing values only do something visible when there is extra width left over: SpaceBetween puts equal gaps between children with none on the outer edges; SpaceAround gives each child equal space on both sides, so edge gaps are half the size of the gaps between children; SpaceEvenly makes every gap — including the two edges — identical. To add a fixed gap between every child regardless of leftover space, use Arrangement.spacedBy(8.dp); you can even combine spacing with alignment via Arrangement.spacedBy(8.dp, Alignment.CenterHorizontally).

Cross axis — verticalAlignment. Because children can have different heights, verticalAlignment chooses where each one is parked within the row's measured height. The three values are Alignment.Top (default), Alignment.CenterVertically, and Alignment.Bottom. This is a single value applied to all children, but any individual child can override it with Modifier.align(Alignment.CenterVertically) inside RowScope. The row's own height defaults to wrapping its tallest child, so alignment matters most when child heights differ or when you give the row a fixed/expanded height.

Modifier.weight — proportional widths. Inside RowScope, every child can call Modifier.weight(weight: Float, fill: Boolean = true). Weighted children are laid out after the unweighted ones are measured: the remaining horizontal space is divided among them in proportion to their weights. Two children with weight(1f) each take half the leftover width; a weight(2f) child takes twice as much as a weight(1f) child. Weight is the idiomatic way to make a child "fill the rest of the row" or to build fluid, percentage-style layouts that adapt to screen width — far better than hardcoded width values.
