The optional fill argument, when false, lets the child be smaller than its allotted slot (it still reserves the space but won't be forced to expand into it).

Baseline alignment. For rows of text with different font sizes, aligning by top or center looks off because the letters don't sit on a common line. Modifier.alignByBaseline() (in RowScope) aligns children by their first text baseline instead of their box edges, so a large heading and small caption share the same writing line. Use it on the text children you want lined up. There is also Modifier.paddingFrom() / alignmentBy for advanced cases, but alignByBaseline() covers the common "mixed font sizes on one line" need.

Why it behaves this way. Row is a thin, single-pass layout built on Compose's measure/place model: it measures non-weighted children with the incoming constraints, subtracts their widths from the available width, divides the remainder among weighted children, then places everything according to the arrangement and alignment. This is why weighted children "see" only leftover space, why arrangements like SpaceBetween need slack to be visible, and why an unconstrained-width Row (e.g. inside a horizontally scrolling parent) treats arrangement as a no-op — there is no extra space to distribute.
key_points: Row stacks children horizontally in declaration order; defaults are horizontalArrangement = Arrangement.Start and verticalAlignment = Alignment.Top.
horizontalArrangement controls the MAIN (horizontal) axis: position (Start/End/Center) or space distribution (SpaceBetween/SpaceAround/SpaceEvenly).
verticalAlignment controls the CROSS (vertical) axis with Top, CenterVertically, or Bottom; override per child with Modifier.align(...).
SpaceBetween/SpaceAround/SpaceEvenly only show a visible effect when the Row has leftover width beyond its children.
Arrangement.spacedBy(dp) adds a fixed gap between every child and can be combined with an alignment argument.
Modifier.weight(f) divides the row's REMAINING width among weighted children in proportion; weighted children are measured after fixed-size ones.
weight(f, fill = false) reserves proportional space but lets the child stay smaller than its slot instead of expanding to fill it.
Modifier.alignByBaseline() lines up text children by their first text baseline, ideal for mixing font sizes on one line.
By default a Row's height wraps its tallest child and its width wraps its content unless you add fillMaxWidth/weight or fixed sizes.
gotchas_html:
Common mistakes with Row:

  • Expecting SpaceBetween to work without slack. If the Row only wraps its content (no fillMaxWidth, no fixed width), there is no extra space, so SpaceBetween/SpaceAround/SpaceEvenly look identical to Start. Add Modifier.fillMaxWidth() (or a width) so the arrangement has room to distribute.
  • Confusing the two axes. In a Row, horizontalArrangement is the main axis and verticalAlignment is the cross axis — the exact opposite of Column. There is no horizontalAlignment parameter on Row and no verticalArrangement; reaching for them is a sign you've mixed up the container.
  • Using weight outside RowScope. weight(), align(), and alignByBaseline() are RowScope extensions; they won't compile outside a Row/Column lambda. Also, calling fillMaxWidth() on a child instead of weight() makes that one child eat all the width and squeeze siblings to zero.
  • Adding gaps with padding instead of spacedBy. Putting padding on each child creates uneven double-gaps and edge padding you may not want. Prefer Arrangement.spacedBy(8.dp) for clean, uniform inter-child spacing.
  • Aligning mixed-size text by center. CenterVertically centers the boxes, not the text baselines, so different font sizes look misaligned. Use Modifier.alignByBaseline() on the text children instead.
  • Forgetting horizontal overflow doesn't wrap. A Row never wraps to a second line; children that overflow are simply clipped or pushed off-screen. For wrapping behavior use FlowRow instead.

playground.intro: Drag the controls to see how a Row positions three children. horizontalArrangement spreads them along the main (horizontal) axis, verticalAlignment parks them on the cross (vertical) axis, gap adds fixed spacing, and padding insets the whole Row.

title: Box
summary: Box stacks its children on top of one another in z-order (declaration order, last drawn on top). contentAlignment positions every child within the Box, Modifier.align overrides alignment per child, and Modifier.matchParentSize makes a child match the Box's resolved size without influencing it. Ideal for overlays, badges, and backgrounds.
explanation_html:
Box is Compose's stacking container. Unlike Column (vertical) and Row (horizontal), a Box places all of its children in the same space, layered on top of one another. The order is simply the order you declare them: the first child is drawn at the bottom, and each subsequent child is painted on top, so the last composable in the block ends up on top. This z-ordering is purely positional — there is no zIndex required for the common case (though Modifier.zIndex() exists to override it). This makes Box the natural tool for overlays, badges, scrims, loading spinners over content, and putting text on top of an image.

Sizing: Box wraps its largest child. By default a Box measures every child with the incoming constraints and then sizes itself to fit the widest and tallest child (i.e. the union of their sizes), much like WrapContent. Smaller children are then positioned within that resolved box according to alignment. If you give the Box an explicit size (Modifier.size, fillMaxSize, etc.), that size wins and children are laid out inside it.

contentAlignment aligns ALL children at once. The contentAlignment parameter takes an Alignment (a 2-D alignment combining a horizontal and vertical bias) and applies it to every child that does not specify its own alignment. The default is Alignment.TopStart, which is why children pile up in the top-left corner unless you say otherwise. The nine common values — TopStart, TopCenter, TopEnd, CenterStart, Center, CenterEnd, BottomStart, BottomCenter, BottomEnd — cover the grid; Start/End are RTL-aware (they flip in right-to-left layouts), whereas the absolute variants like Alignment.TopRight do not.

Modifier.align overrides alignment per child. Inside a BoxScope, each child can call Modifier.align(Alignment.BottomEnd) to position itself independently of the Box's contentAlignment. This is the badge pattern: a Box might center its main content while a small badge pins itself to TopEnd. The per-child align always wins over the container's contentAlignment for that specific child.

Modifier.matchParentSize sizes a child to the Box — without affecting the Box's size. This BoxScope-only modifier is the key to backgrounds and scrims. A child with Modifier.matchParentSize() is forced to the Box's final resolved size, but crucially it is not measured first when the Box decides how big to be. Contrast with Modifier.fillMaxSize(): fillMaxSize expands to the incoming constraints (which, in a wrap-content Box, can cause the Box to blow up to fill its parent), whereas matchParentSize says "be exactly as big as whatever the other children make this Box." So a background that should match the content uses matchParentSize(), declared first so it sits behind everything.

Box vs. BoxWithConstraints. A plain Box cannot read its own measured size during composition. When you need the resolved constraints (e.g. to switch layout based on available width), reach for BoxWithConstraints, whose scope exposes maxWidth, minWidth, maxHeight, etc. — but it is slightly more expensive because it defers content composition until measurement, so prefer plain Box when you don't need those values.
key_points: Box stacks all children in the same space; z-order follows declaration order — the LAST child is drawn on top.
By default Box sizes itself to wrap its largest child (union of children's sizes), unless you give it an explicit size.
contentAlignment positions every child that doesn't override it; the default is Alignment.TopStart (top-left).
Modifier.align(Alignment.X) is a BoxScope modifier that overrides contentAlignment for one child — the badge pattern.
Modifier.matchParentSize() forces a child to the Box's final size WITHOUT contributing to how big the Box becomes.
fillMaxSize() expands to the incoming constraints; matchParentSize() matches the Box's resolved content size — they are not the same.
Start/End and Center alignments are RTL-aware; absolute Left/Right variants are not.
Use BoxWithConstraints (not plain Box) when you need to read maxWidth/maxHeight during composition.
An empty Box with a size/background is a common, cheap way to draw a colored rectangle or spacer.
gotchas_html:
Confusing matchParentSize with fillMaxSize. This is the number-one Box mistake. In a wrap-content Box, Modifier.fillMaxSize() on the background child expands to the parent's constraints, which often makes the whole Box (and its content) balloon to fill the screen. Use Modifier.matchParentSize() for a background that should hug the other children, and declare it first so it draws underneath.

  • Wrong stacking order. If your overlay or badge disappears, remember the first child is at the bottom. Backgrounds go first; overlays, scrims, and badges go last. Reordering the composables is how you reorder layers (or use Modifier.zIndex() to override without moving code).
  • Expecting contentAlignment to move an already-aligned child. A child with its own Modifier.align(...) ignores the Box's contentAlignment. Don't set contentAlignment = Center and wonder why a BottomEnd-aligned badge didn't move.
  • Calling align/matchParentSize outside a Box. Modifier.align and Modifier.matchParentSize are BoxScope extensions — they won't compile in a Column/Row context (Column/Row have their own align overloads that behave differently).
  • Assuming Box needs zIndex for layering. It doesn't — declaration order already defines layers. zIndex is only for the rare case where you must paint a child on top without changing its position in the code.
  • Using a Box to lay items out side-by-side. Box overlaps children; it does not arrange them in a line. For that you want Row or Column. A Box of two visible children that "overlap weirdly" usually means you reached for the wrong container.

playground.intro: A Box with three stacked children. Change contentAlignment to move all the children at once, and add padding to inset them from the Box edges. Notice how they pile up in the same space rather than sitting side by side.

title: Custom layouts: measure, place & intrinsics
summary: When Row, Column, Box, Lazy layouts, and modifiers cannot express a layout rule, Compose lets you write the measure/place policy yourself with Modifier.layout or Layout. The contract is strict: measure children, choose the parent size, then place every child exactly where it belongs.
explanation_html:
Custom layout is the point where Compose stops being a set of ready-made containers and becomes a layout engine you can program directly. The core model is the same one the built-ins use: a parent receives constraints, measures children within those constraints, chooses its own size, then places the measured children. The three steps are not optional: measure children, decide size, place children. A child that is measured but never placed is invisible, and a parent size that ignores incoming constraints can break the layout contract.

Use Modifier.layout when you want to change the measurement or placement of one composable from the outside. A classic example is baseline padding: measure the text once, read its FirstBaseline alignment line from the resulting Placeable, choose a taller parent height, then place the text lower so the baseline lands at the requested distance from the top. This is how you build a reusable modifier-shaped layout policy.

Use the Layout composable when the parent owns multiple children and their positions depend on one another. Layout gives you a list of Measurable children and the incoming Constraints. Each Measurable can be measured once in that layout pass to produce a Placeable. From those Placeables you compute width, height, and placement coordinates. Higher-level containers such as Row and Column are built on this same primitive.

The single-pass measurement rule is the boundary that makes Compose layout predictable and fast: you cannot measure the same child repeatedly to try different layouts. If a parent needs information before measurement, use intrinsic measurements such as IntrinsicSize.Min, or override the intrinsic methods of a custom MeasurePolicy when the default approximation is wrong. Intrinsics are a pre-measure query; they are not a loophole for running measurement twice.

For text and other reference-line layouts, use alignment lines. Built-in text exposes FirstBaseline and LastBaseline; custom layouts can expose their own AlignmentLine values in the layout(width, height, alignmentLines = ...) call. Parents can read those values from Placeables and align siblings around semantic coordinates instead of raw edges. Use placeRelative unless you intentionally want absolute left/right placement, because placeRelative respects layout direction.
key_points: Every custom layout follows the same three-step contract: measure children, decide parent size, place children.
Use Modifier.layout for a one-child measurement or placement policy; use Layout when the parent owns multiple children.
A Measurable becomes a Placeable after measure(constraints); Placeable has width, height, alignment lines, and placement functions.
Compose does not permit measuring the same child more than once in one layout pass.
A measured child is not visible until it is placed inside the layout(width, height) placement block.
Use placeRelative for direction-aware placement; use place only for deliberate absolute coordinates.
Use intrinsic measurements when the parent needs a child's size information before actual measurement.
Use alignment lines such as FirstBaseline, LastBaseline, or custom AlignmentLine values when parents should align to semantic reference lines.
gotchas_html:
• Measuring the same child twice. Compose layout is single-pass. Measuring a Measurable once for width and again for height can throw at runtime. Decide constraints first, use intrinsics for pre-measure questions, or restructure the layout.
  • Forgetting to place a Placeable. val placeable = measurable.measure(...) only computes size. The child will not appear unless the placement block calls place or placeRelative.
  • Ignoring incoming constraints. Returning a width or height outside the allowed range can produce broken parent-child contracts. Coerce computed sizes into the given constraints.
  • Using place when you meant RTL-aware placement. placeRelative mirrors start/end positioning for right-to-left layouts; place is absolute x/y.
  • Writing a custom layout for a built-in problem. Row, Column, Box, FlowRow, lazy grids, Scaffold, constraints, and modifiers cover most screens. Custom Layout is for a real measurement or placement contract, not for style.
  • Forgetting intrinsics after custom measurement. Default intrinsic approximations may be wrong for unusual custom layouts. Override intrinsic methods when parents using IntrinsicSize need correct answers.
  • Hiding semantic alignment in raw numbers. If a parent should align to a baseline, chart value, or other meaningful coordinate, expose an AlignmentLine instead of making every parent duplicate coordinate math.

playground.intro: Use the Column playground as the built-in baseline, then compare it to the custom Layout code. A handmade Column measures each child once, sums the heights, chooses a parent size, and places children top-to-bottom.

title: ConstraintLayout in Compose: refs, ConstraintSet, guidelines, barriers and chains
summary: ConstraintLayout exists in Compose for sibling-relative positioning, guidelines, barriers, chains, and swappable constraint sets, but Compose no longer needs it just to flatten a hierarchy. Reach for it when the constraint graph is clearer than Row, Column, Box, Flow, or a custom Layout.
explanation_html:
ConstraintLayout in Compose is a separate layout tool from androidx.constraintlayout:constraintlayout-compose. It lets children position themselves relative to the parent, siblings, guidelines, barriers, and chains. That makes it useful for constraint-heavy screens, migrated View layouts, adaptive positions that swap between multiple constraint sets, and UIs where sibling edges are the natural language of the design.

The important Compose-specific rule is that ConstraintLayout is no longer a performance default. In the View system, flattening a complex hierarchy was often a reason to choose ConstraintLayout. Compose handles nested Row, Column, Box, lazy containers, and custom layouts efficiently, so choose ConstraintLayout for clarity of constraints, not because nesting is automatically bad.

The inline API uses createRefs() or createRef(), then each child gets Modifier.constrainAs(ref) { ... }. Links such as top.linkTo(parent.top, margin = 16.dp), start.linkTo(avatar.end), and end.linkTo(action.start) build the graph. Use Dimension.fillToConstraints when a child should occupy the space between two linked edges.

The decoupled API moves constraints into a ConstraintSet. Children use Modifier.layoutId("name"), and the ConstraintSet creates matching references with createRefFor("name"). This is the right shape when constraints change by window width, orientation, feature state, or animation. Keep those IDs in sync; a mismatch means the emitted child has no matching constraints.

ConstraintLayout concepts have Compose alternatives. Guidelines can often be a Spacer, padding, BoxWithConstraints, or a weighted Row/Column. Barriers can sometimes be expressed with intrinsic measurements. Chains map to Row or Column plus Arrangement in many cases. If the alternative is clearer, use it. If the graph is easier to read as constraints, use ConstraintLayout intentionally and test long text, RTL, font scale, and compact/expanded widths.
key_points: Use ConstraintLayout for constraint graphs, not as a default replacement for nested Compose layouts.
Add the constraintlayout-compose dependency separately from Compose UI and Material dependencies.
Inline constraints use createRefs or createRef plus Modifier.constrainAs(ref).
Use parent to constrain a child to the ConstraintLayout itself.
Use Dimension.fillToConstraints when a child should fill between linked edges.
Decoupled constraints use ConstraintSet, createRefFor("id"), and matching Modifier.layoutId("id") values.
ConstraintSet is useful when constraints change by window size, state, or animation.
Guidelines are virtual dp or percentage anchors; consider Spacer, padding, or BoxWithConstraints first.
Barriers align to the most extreme edge of multiple children; consider intrinsic measurements for ordinary Row/Column alternatives.
Chains distribute children along one axis with ChainStyle.Spread, SpreadInside, or Packed; Row/Column Arrangement is often simpler.
Test ConstraintLayout screens with long text, RTL, font scale, and compact/expanded constraints because edge links can hide truncation bugs.
gotchas_html:
• Using ConstraintLayout to flatten hierarchy by habit. Compose can handle nested layouts efficiently. Prefer constraints only when the graph is easier to understand.
  • Unconstrained children. A child inside ConstraintLayout should have constrainAs or a layoutId that matches the active ConstraintSet.
  • Mismatched layoutId strings. createRefFor("title") does not bind to Modifier.layoutId("headline"). Use constants for larger decoupled layouts.
  • Forgetting width/height dimensions. If a child is constrained between start and end but should fill that span, set width = Dimension.fillToConstraints.
  • Overusing guidelines. A percentage line is sometimes right, but padding, a spacer, a weight, or BoxWithConstraints can be more readable.
  • Barrier where intrinsic layout is enough. If you only need a divider or row to match text height, an intrinsic Row may be simpler.
  • Chain where Arrangement is clearer. For ordinary evenly spaced buttons or chips, Row(horizontalArrangement = Arrangement.SpaceBetween) is easier to scan.
  • Ignoring adaptive variants. Decoupled ConstraintSet layouts should be verified in compact and expanded widths, not just the current emulator size.

playground.intro: Switch between inline refs, decoupled ConstraintSet, guidelines, barriers, and chains. Keep the Compose alternative visible to decide whether ConstraintLayout is actually clearer than Row, Column, Box, or custom Layout for the same problem.

title: Visibility Tracking: onVisibilityChanged, onLayoutRectChanged and viewport-aware work
summary: Visibility callbacks let Compose code react to what is actually inside a viewport. Use onVisibilityChanged for common feed impressions, autoplay, and start/stop work; use onLayoutRectChanged only when you need raw RelativeLayoutBounds, throttling, debouncing, custom viewport math, or occlusion-aware geometry.
explanation_html:
Modern Compose has first-class visibility tracking for the cases that used to require fragile onGloballyPositioned math. The high-level tool is Modifier.onVisibilityChanged: attach it to a node, choose a visibility threshold, and the callback receives true or false when that node crosses the threshold inside the window or a supplied viewport. That makes it a good fit for feed impressions, autoplaying videos, pausing animations, starting a lightweight prefetch, or reporting when important content is genuinely seen.

Two parameters decide whether a visible callback is meaningful. minFractionVisible says how much of the node must be visible before it counts; 1f means the whole node, 0.5f means half, and 0f means any non-zero pixels. minDurationMs says how long the node must stay visible before true is emitted. Impression analytics usually need both: for example, at least half the card visible for one second. Autoplay might choose a higher fraction and shorter duration, while a sticky toolbar state might use a lower duration but still avoid rapid flicker.

The lower-level tool is Modifier.onLayoutRectChanged. Its callback receives RelativeLayoutBounds, which exposes bounds in root, window, and screen coordinates, plus helpers such as fractionVisibleInWindow(), fractionVisibleInRect(...), fractionVisibleInWindowWithInsets(...), and calculateOcclusions(). Use it when a boolean is not enough: scroll-linked effects, custom viewport calculations, occlusion-aware video decisions, or telemetry that samples position. Because bounds can change at scroll-frame frequency, configure throttleMillis and/or debounceMillis instead of doing heavy work on every layout movement.

A viewport is not always the raw window. A bottom bar, top app bar, drawer scrim, or overlay may cover content that technically sits inside the window. For that case, create a LayoutBoundsHolder, write the visible container bounds with Modifier.layoutBounds(holder), and pass that holder as viewportBounds to onVisibilityChanged. This keeps the definition of visible aligned with the actual content viewport, not just the device window.

Do not use deprecated onFirstVisible() for new code. The Foundation release notes explain that the name is misleading, especially in lazy lists where an item can fire again after scrolling away and back. If the product requirement is "log once ever for this item," use onVisibilityChanged() and track a remembered or model-owned seen flag keyed by stable item identity. The callback says whether the node is visible; your state decides whether that visibility should trigger once, repeatedly, or only while the screen lifecycle is active.
key_points: Use onVisibilityChanged for the common boolean question: is this node visible enough in the viewport?
Set minFractionVisible deliberately; the default full-visibility mental model is often too strict for feed impressions.
Set minDurationMs for analytics and autoplay so fast scroll-throughs do not count as meaningful exposure.
Use onLayoutRectChanged only when you need RelativeLayoutBounds, raw geometry, occlusion checks, custom visibility math, throttling, or debouncing.
RelativeLayoutBounds exposes bounds in root, window, and screen coordinates plus fractionVisibleInWindow and fractionVisibleInRect helpers.
Throttle or debounce onLayoutRectChanged callbacks when scrolling can move the node every frame.
Use LayoutBoundsHolder and layoutBounds when a custom viewport, app bar, bottom bar, or overlay changes what should count as visible.
In lazy lists, keep stable item keys and key any once-only remembered state by item id.
Avoid deprecated onFirstVisible for new code; use onVisibilityChanged plus your own seen flag when the requirement is first-seen behavior.
Keep heavy work out of visibility callbacks. Emit a small event to a state holder, analytics adapter, player controller, or coroutine owner.
gotchas_html:
• Logging from composition instead of visibility. A composable entering composition is not the same as a user seeing it; lazy prefetch and beyond-bounds layout can compose items before they are visible.
  • Using onFirstVisible() for once-only analytics. That API is deprecated because its name can be misleading in lazy lists. Track a seen flag yourself with onVisibilityChanged().
  • Counting a one-pixel flash as an impression. Choose a sensible minFractionVisible and minDurationMs; otherwise fast scrolling or partial peeks inflate metrics.
  • Doing expensive work inside every geometry callback. onLayoutRectChanged can fire frequently during scroll. Throttle/debounce and send compact events rather than allocating, logging, or loading media inline.
  • Ignoring overlays. Content under a bottom bar may be inside the window but not actually visible. Use viewportBounds when an overlay changes the meaningful viewport.
  • Remembering once-only flags without item identity. In lazy lists, key state and lazy items by stable IDs so recycled/composed content does not inherit the wrong seen status.
  • Starting lifecycle-sensitive work while the screen is stopped. Visibility is about layout, not Activity lifecycle. Gate player, camera, or network work through the appropriate lifecycle/state holder when needed.

playground.intro: Pick the tracking shape and tune the visibility threshold. The preview simulates a card crossing the viewport: when the visible fraction and duration clear the contract, the generated Kotlin logs or controls work from a visibility callback instead of from composition.

title: Arrangement Deep-Dive: Distributing Main-Axis Space
summary: Arrangement controls how a Row or Column distributes leftover free space along its main axis — packing children to one end, centering them, or spreading them with SpaceBetween/Around/Evenly, or fixing a gap with spacedBy.
explanation_html:
Arrangement decides what happens to the leftover space along the main axis. Every Row and Column first measures its children, sums their sizes along the main axis (width for a Row, height for a Column), and subtracts that from the space the parent gave it. The remainder is the free space. The horizontalArrangement (Row) or verticalArrangement (Column) parameter is the policy that decides where that free space goes. If children already fill the axis exactly, there is no free space and the arrangement has nothing to redistribute — which is why an arrangement appears to "do nothing" when the container wraps its content tightly. The cross-axis is governed by a separate parameter (verticalAlignment / horizontalAlignment), so arrangement and alignment are orthogonal.

The "packed" arrangements push all children together and place the free space on one side. Arrangement.Start / Arrangement.Top put children at the beginning with all free space after them; Arrangement.End / Arrangement.Bottom put them at the end with all free space before; Arrangement.Center puts equal free space before and after, keeping the block centered. In all three, children remain edge-to-edge with zero gap between them. Start respects layout direction — in an RTL locale, Start means the right edge — whereas Arrangement.Left and Arrangement.Right are absolute and ignore RTL (they only exist for horizontal arrangement and require you to pass a LayoutDirection in custom usage).

The "spread" arrangements divide the free space into the gaps between and around children. Think of it as how the leftover is sliced:

  • SpaceBetween puts zero space on the outer edges and divides all free space into the (n−1) gaps between children — first child flush to the start, last child flush to the end.
  • SpaceEvenly makes every gap equal: the n+1 spaces (before, between, and after) are all the same width.
  • SpaceAround gives each child an equal margin on both sides; because adjacent margins add up, the inner gaps are twice the outer gaps (the outer edges get a half-gap each). Numerically, if free space is F and there are n children: SpaceBetween gap = F/(n−1); SpaceEvenly gap = F/(n+1); SpaceAround outer = F/(2n), inner = F/n.
With a single child, SpaceBetween/Around/Evenly all degrade gracefully — SpaceBetween and SpaceEvenly center it, SpaceAround centers it too.

Arrangement.spacedBy(dp) is a different beast: it adds a fixed gap, not a redistribution of free space. It inserts exactly dp of space between adjacent children (n−1 gaps), and that gap is reserved during measurement, so it shrinks the space available to weighted/fill children rather than consuming leftover space afterward. There is an overload spacedBy(space, alignment) that also aligns the packed block (e.g. spacedBy(8.dp, Alignment.End) pins the spaced block to the end). Because the gap is a fixed dimension, spacedBy(0.dp) equals Start/Top. This is the idiomatic way to add uniform spacing between list items instead of putting padding on each child.

Arrangement is strongly typed by axis. horizontalArrangement wants an Arrangement.Horizontal, verticalArrangement wants an Arrangement.Vertical. Center, SpaceBetween, SpaceAround, SpaceEvenly, and spacedBy implement Arrangement.HorizontalOrVertical, so they work in both. But the end-anchored ones are axis-specific: Start/End are horizontal-only, Top/Bottom are vertical-only — passing Arrangement.Top to a Row won't compile. The defaults are Arrangement.Start for Row and Arrangement.Top for Column, matching reading order.
key_points: Arrangement distributes only the LEFTOVER main-axis free space; if children fill the axis (or the container wraps content) there is nothing to distribute.
Arrangement = main axis; Alignment = cross axis. They are independent parameters.
Start/Top/End/Bottom/Center keep children edge-to-edge and move the whole block; only the spread modes add gaps between children.
SpaceBetween = no outer space, free space split into the n−1 inner gaps. SpaceEvenly = all n+1 gaps equal. SpaceAround = each child gets equal margin, so inner gaps are 2× the outer gaps.
Arrangement.spacedBy(dp) adds a FIXED gap and is reserved during measurement, so it reduces space for fill/weight children — it does not redistribute leftover space.
Center, SpaceBetween, SpaceAround, SpaceEvenly, and spacedBy are HorizontalOrVertical and work on both axes; Start/End are horizontal-only and Top/Bottom are vertical-only.
Start/End/Top/Bottom respect LayoutDirection (RTL); Arrangement.Left/Right are absolute and ignore RTL.
Defaults: Row uses Arrangement.Start, Column uses Arrangement.Top.
spacedBy(space, alignment) both spaces and anchors the packed block; spacedBy(0.dp) == Start/Top.
gotchas_html:
The single most common confusion is "my arrangement does nothing." That almost always means there is no free space to distribute:

  • A Row with no Modifier.fillMaxWidth() (or a fixed width) wraps its content, so width == sum of children and free space is zero. SpaceBetween looks identical to Start. Fix: give the container a width with fillMaxWidth() or a fixed width.
  • A child with Modifier.weight(1f) expands to eat all remaining space, again leaving zero free space for the arrangement. Weight and arrangement compete for the same leftover; weight wins.

Other frequent mistakes:

  • Confusing SpaceAround with SpaceEvenly. SpaceAround's outer margins are half the inner gaps, so it looks lopsided compared to the perfectly uniform SpaceEvenly. They are not interchangeable.
  • Expecting spacedBy to behave like the spread modes. spacedBy is a fixed gap that can push content out of bounds if there isn't room; it never grows to fill extra space. Conversely SpaceBetween's gap collapses to zero when content is tight.
  • Using padding on each child to fake spacing. This double-counts at the outer edges and is brittle; prefer Arrangement.spacedBy().
  • Mixing up the axis. Setting verticalArrangement on a Row (or horizontalArrangement on a Column) controls the cross axis stacking only for the single-line case and is usually not what you want — for a Row use horizontalArrangement + verticalAlignment.
  • Assuming Start means left. Under RTL, Arrangement.Start resolves to the right; use Arrangement.Left/Right only when you truly want absolute, direction-agnostic placement.

playground.intro: This Row fills its width, so there is real free space to distribute. Change the horizontalArrangement to watch the leftover space snap to the start/end/center or spread between the children. Then drag childCount up and down and notice how SpaceBetween, SpaceAround, and SpaceEvenly each re-slice the gaps differently as items come and go.

title: Alignment Deep-Dive
summary: Alignment controls cross-axis positioning: where a child sits perpendicular to the layout's main flow. Box uses the full 2D Alignment (9 values), Column uses Alignment.Horizontal, Row uses Alignment.Vertical, and baselines let text rows line up by their letters.
explanation_html:
Alignment is the cross-axis half of layout. Every layout has a main axis (controlled by Arrangement) and a cross axis (controlled by Alignment). In a Column the main axis is vertical, so alignment decides horizontal position. In a Row the main axis is horizontal, so alignment decides vertical position. A Box has no flow direction at all — it stacks children on top of one another — so it uses a full two-dimensional Alignment that positions on both axes at once. Crucially, alignment only does something when there is extra space on the cross axis: a child that already fills the cross axis has nowhere to move, so the alignment value is a no-op for it.

The three Alignment types are deliberately distinct and not interchangeable. The 2D Alignment interface (used by Box and by the Modifier.align inside a Box) has nine canonical constants: TopStart, TopCenter, TopEnd, CenterStart, Center, CenterEnd, BottomStart, BottomCenter, BottomEnd. Column takes an Alignment.Horizontal — only Start, CenterHorizontally, or End. Row takes an Alignment.Vertical — only Top, CenterVertically, or Bottom. The compiler enforces this: you cannot pass Alignment.Center (the 2D one) to a Column's horizontalAlignment parameter. This separation exists because a Column already owns the vertical axis via its arrangement, so it only lets you choose the one free dimension.

Defaults matter and they are not all "center." A bare Column defaults to horizontalAlignment = Alignment.Start, and a bare Row defaults to verticalAlignment = Alignment.Top — children hug the start/top edge, not the center. A Box, however, defaults to contentAlignment = Alignment.TopStart. These container-level alignments set the default for all children, but any single child can override it with the per-child modifier: Modifier.align(...). The scope determines what's legal — inside BoxScope you use the 2D align, inside ColumnScope you use Modifier.align(Alignment.Horizontal), inside RowScope you use Modifier.align(Alignment.Vertical).

Start/End are layout-direction-aware; Left/Right are not. Alignment.Start resolves to the left in left-to-right locales and to the right in right-to-left (RTL) locales, because it respects LocalLayoutDirection. The 2D Alignment also exposes raw Alignment.TopStart etc. which are direction-aware, while the absolute variants under Alignment.Absolute (e.g. Alignment.Absolute.TopLeft) ignore direction. Prefer the direction-aware ones unless you have a deliberate reason to pin to physical left/right. Internally every Alignment is just a function: given a child size and a parent size, it returns an IntOffset for where to place the child — which is why you can build custom alignments with BiasAlignment or the Alignment { size, space, layoutDirection -> ... } factory.

Baselines are a special, text-aware kind of alignment. Aligning by edges (top/center/bottom) lines up the boxes of two pieces of text, but if they have different font sizes their letters won't sit on the same line. Modifier.alignByBaseline() inside a Row aligns children by their first text baseline instead — the invisible line the letters rest on — so a large headline and a small caption read as one typographic line. This works through alignment lines (FirstBaseline and LastBaseline), a general mechanism where a child reports named horizontal/vertical reference lines that a parent can align siblings to. You can also align to a baseline measured from a specific child using Modifier.alignBy(FirstBaseline) / alignBy(LastBaseline), or define custom alignment lines for advanced layouts.
key_points: Arrangement controls the main axis; Alignment controls the cross axis. They never overlap.
Box uses the 2D Alignment (9 values); Column uses Alignment.Horizontal (Start/CenterHorizontally/End); Row uses Alignment.Vertical (Top/CenterVertically/Bottom). The types are not interchangeable.
Defaults: Column → Start, Row → Top, Box → TopStart. Only Box centers nothing by default and is the only 2D one.
Alignment only moves a child when there is leftover cross-axis space; a child filling the cross axis ignores its alignment.
Container alignment sets the default for all children; per-child Modifier.align(...) overrides it for one child within the matching scope.
Start/End and the standard 2D constants are layout-direction (RTL) aware; Alignment.Absolute.* and Left/Right ignore direction.
An Alignment is a function (child size, parent size, direction) → IntOffset, so custom alignments are possible via BiasAlignment or the Alignment {} factory.
Modifier.alignByBaseline() (and alignBy(FirstBaseline/LastBaseline)) align Row children by their text baseline, not their box edges.
gotchas_html:
The most common alignment mistakes come from confusing the axis or the type:

  • Passing the wrong Alignment type. You cannot put Alignment.Center on a Column's horizontalAlignment — it wants Alignment.CenterHorizontally. Likewise a Row wants Alignment.CenterVertically, not Center. The naming is intentional so the axis is explicit.
  • Expecting alignment to center on the main axis. horizontalAlignment on a Column will not center children vertically — that's verticalArrangement = Arrangement.Center. Mixing these up is the #1 confusion.
  • Aligning a child that already fills the cross axis. If a Column child has Modifier.fillMaxWidth(), no horizontal alignment will appear to do anything because the child occupies all the width. Alignment needs slack to work with.
  • Assuming Box centers by default. Box defaults to TopStart, not Center. To center content you must explicitly set contentAlignment = Alignment.Center (or align the child).
  • Using edge alignment for mixed-size text. Aligning differently-sized texts by Bottom or CenterVertically makes their boxes line up but their letters drift off the line. Use Modifier.alignByBaseline() instead.
  • Forgetting align lives in scope. Modifier.align(...) only compiles inside BoxScope/ColumnScope/RowScope — it's a scoped extension, not a global modifier, and the available values depend on the scope.
  • Reaching for Left/Right and breaking RTL. Prefer Start/End; the absolute Left/Right variants silently ignore right-to-left locales.

playground.intro: A Box stacks its child on top of itself. With no main-axis flow, alignment is fully two-dimensional — pick any of the 9 positions and watch the child snap to that corner, edge, or center. Add extra space (a bigger Box, a smaller child) and the alignment has room to move.

title: Weight & fill
summary: Modifier.weight() splits the leftover space along a Row's or Column's main axis in proportion to each child's weight, after the non-weighted children have been measured.
explanation_html:
Modifier.weight(weight: Float, fill: Boolean = true) is a RowScope / ColumnScope modifier — it only exists inside a Row or a Column (and a couple of related scopes like FlowRow). It tells the parent how to share the main-axis space: horizontal space in a Row, vertical space in a Column. It has no effect on the cross axis and does not exist in a Box.

The measurement happens in two passes, and this order is the whole mental model. First, the parent measures every non-weighted child with the normal constraints and adds up how much main-axis space they consume (including any Arrangement.spacedBy gaps). Second, it takes the remaining space — the parent's main-axis size minus what the fixed children already took — and divides it among the weighted children in proportion to their weights. A child's slot is remaining × (thisWeight / sumOfAllWeights). So weight is about proportions of leftover space, never about absolute size, and the proportion always depends on the other weighted siblings.

Concretely, three children with weights 1f, 1f, 2f in a fixed-width Row divide the leftover into quarters: 1/4, 1/4, 2/4. If you change them to 1f, 1f, 1f you get equal thirds. The absolute numbers are irrelevant — 1f/1f/1f behaves identically to 10f/10f/10f; only the ratios matter. This is why a single weighted child (e.g. a spacer with Modifier.weight(1f)) simply eats all the remaining space, which is the idiomatic way to push siblings apart.

The second parameter, fill, defaults to true and controls whether the child is forced to occupy its whole computed slot. With fill = true the child receives a fixed main-axis constraint equal to its slot — minWidth equals maxWidth — so it stretches to exactly fill it even if its content is smaller. With fill = false the slot becomes a maximum: the child may measure smaller and only takes what it needs, but it can never exceed the slot, and any space it leaves unused is simply… left unused (it is not redistributed to the other weighted children, because the division already happened). Use fill = false when you want a cap (a button that can grow up to its share but shrink to wrap its text) rather than a forced stretch.

A weight of 0f is a documented special case: the child contributes nothing to the weight sum and effectively behaves like a non-weighted child measured at its intrinsic / wrap-content size, so it is laid out in the first pass. Passing a negative or NaN weight throws IllegalArgumentException — Compose requires weight >= 0. Also note weight is a hard split of available space, so if the parent is wrapContent on the main axis (unbounded constraints), there is no "remaining space" to divide and weighted children collapse — weight needs a parent with a bounded main-axis size to be meaningful.
key_points: weight() lives in RowScope/ColumnScope only; it splits the MAIN axis (horizontal in Row, vertical in Column) and does nothing in a Box.
Non-weighted children are measured first; weighted children then divide the LEFTOVER space, not the total.
A child's slot = remaining × (itsWeight / sum of all weights) — only ratios matter, so 1f/1f/2f == 10f/10f/20f.
fill defaults to true: the child is given a FIXED constraint and stretches to fill its whole slot.
fill = false makes the slot a maximum: the child may measure smaller, and leftover space is NOT redistributed.
weight 0f means the child takes its intrinsic/wrap-content size and is measured in the first pass.
Negative or NaN weight throws IllegalArgumentException; weight must be >= 0.
Arrangement.spacedBy gaps are subtracted before division, so gaps shrink the pool the weights share.
Weight needs a bounded main axis; in a wrapContent/unbounded parent there is no remaining space to divide.
gotchas_html:
The most common mistakes with weight:

  • Thinking weight is a fraction of the total. Weight divides the space left after the fixed children are measured. Add a wide non-weighted icon and your "weight(1f) = full width" child suddenly gets less than you expected.
  • Expecting fill = false to give its leftover to siblings. It doesn't. The split is computed up front from the weights; a non-filling child that measures small just leaves empty space in its own slot. If you want true content-based sharing, use intrinsics or arrangement instead.
  • Using weight in a Box or on the cross axis. weight() simply isn't in BoxScope, and inside a Row it never affects height. To stretch across the cross axis use Modifier.fillMaxHeight() / fillMaxWidth() or align.
  • Putting weight on a child inside a scrolling Row/Column. A scrollable axis is unbounded, so there is no finite "remaining" space — weighted children collapse to zero. Weight and verticalScroll/horizontalScroll on the same axis don't mix.
  • Assuming absolute weight values matter. weight(2f) isn't "200dp" or "twice the screen" — it's twice the share of whatever its weighted siblings get. With no siblings it just takes everything.
  • Passing 0f hoping the child disappears. Weight 0f means wrap-content, not zero size; the child still measures at its intrinsic size.

playground.intro: Each child has a weight slider (0–3). The Row has a fixed width, so the children split the space left over after any weight-0 (wrap-content) children are measured. Set a child to 0 to see it shrink to its label; raise another to watch its share grow proportionally.

title: Background, Border, Clip & Shape
summary: Paint, outline, and round your composables with background, border, and clip — three modifiers that all take a Shape, and whose order in the chain decides exactly what you see.
explanation_html:
Three decoration modifiers do most of the visual work in Compose, and they share a common currency: a Shape. Modifier.background() fills a composable's area with a solid Color or a Brush (gradient), Modifier.border() strokes an outline around it, and Modifier.clip() trims the composable — and crucially its children's pixels — to a shape. Each of these draws relative to the element's own bounds at that point in the modifier chain, which is why order is the single most important concept in this topic. A modifier chain is applied outside-in for layout/measurement but each draw modifier paints using the size and position established by the modifiers before it.

The Shape vocabulary. The shape argument types your corners. RectangleShape is the default (sharp 90° corners). RoundedCornerShape rounds them — you can pass one value for all four corners (RoundedCornerShape(12.dp)), per-corner values (RoundedCornerShape(topStart = 16.dp, bottomEnd = 16.dp)), or percentages (RoundedCornerShape(50) = fully rounded ends, a "pill"). CircleShape is literally RoundedCornerShape(50%) and renders a circle only when the element is square; on a rectangle it becomes a stadium/pill. CutCornerShape chamfers corners with straight diagonal cuts instead of arcs. All of these implement the Shape interface, so anywhere one is accepted, any of them works — including your own custom GenericShape.

Units and parameters. Corner sizes are Dp (or an Int/Float percentage of the smaller dimension). border(width: Dp, color: Color, shape: Shape = RectangleShape) takes a stroke width in Dp; there's also a border(BorderStroke, Shape) overload so you can stroke with a Brush gradient. The border is drawn centered on the shape's outline by default — meaning roughly half the stroke sits inside the bounds and half just outside, which matters when you align borders against clips. background() defaults its shape to RectangleShape, so omitting the shape gives you a plain rectangle fill.

Why clip matters and why order is everything. clip(shape) installs a graphics-layer clip so that anything drawn after it — backgrounds, borders, child content, images — is masked to that shape. Put clip before background and the fill is rounded; put it after and the fill is a sharp rectangle that pokes out past the rounded outline. The reliable recipe is clip → background → border → padding → content. Because each shape modifier independently receives a shape argument, a common subtle bug is rounding the background but forgetting to clip, so a child Image spills square corners over your rounded card.

Shadows, elevation, and the clip interaction. Use Modifier.shadow(elevation, shape, clip = false) to cast a shadow that follows the shape; shadow conveniently also clips when you set clip = true. A standalone clip() does not cast a shadow, and a graphicsLayer clip can cut off a shadow drawn after it. The canonical stack for an elevated rounded card is shadow(8.dp, shape) then clip(shape) then background(color, shape) — shadow first (so it escapes the clip), then clip, then fill. In Material 3, prefer Card/Surface with a shape parameter, which wires all of this up correctly for you.
key_points: background(color/brush, shape) fills the element; shape defaults to RectangleShape, so omit it for a plain rectangle.
border(width: Dp, color, shape) strokes an outline; an overload takes BorderStroke so the outline can be a Brush gradient.
clip(shape) masks everything drawn after it — including child content and Images — to that shape; it is the only one of the three that affects children.
The reliable order is clip → background → border → padding → content; rounding the background without clip lets square children spill past the corners.
CircleShape == RoundedCornerShape(50%); it's only a true circle when the element is square, otherwise it's a pill/stadium.
RoundedCornerShape accepts one Dp, per-corner Dp values, or an Int/Float percentage of the smaller side; CutCornerShape chamfers with straight diagonals.
Corner radii are in Dp (or % of the smaller dimension); border width is in Dp and is drawn centered on the outline.
clip() alone casts no shadow; use Modifier.shadow(elevation, shape) before clip so the shadow follows the shape and isn't trimmed away.
Prefer Material 3 Card/Surface with a shape parameter when you want elevation + clipping handled correctly out of the box.
gotchas_html:
Most decoration bugs come from confusing which modifier rounds which layer, and from chain order:

  • Rounded background but square children. background(color, RoundedCornerShape(16.dp)) rounds only the fill. A child Image or colored Box still draws square corners on top. You need clip(RoundedCornerShape(16.dp)) to actually mask the content.
  • Wrong order = sharp corners. background(color, shape).clip(shape) rounds nothing visible — the background was already painted as a rectangle before the clip took effect. Clip must come before background.
  • Border drawn under a clip gets cut in half. Because borders straddle the outline, clipping after the border can shave off its outer half, making it look thin or uneven. Clip first, then border, so the stroke sits cleanly inside the rounded edge.
  • Expecting clip() to cast a shadow. It won't. Rounding a card with only clip gives a flat shape. Add Modifier.shadow(elevation, shape) before the clip — a shadow placed after a clip is trimmed away.
  • CircleShape on a non-square element. Applying CircleShape to a wide rectangle yields a pill, not a circle. Constrain the element with size() (or aspectRatio(1f)) first.
  • Percentage vs Dp confusion. RoundedCornerShape(50) (Int) means 50% and fully rounds; RoundedCornerShape(50.dp) means a fixed 50dp radius. Mixing them up gives wildly different results.
  • Padding placement. Padding before background shrinks the painted area (background doesn't cover the padded margin); padding after it insets the content within the colored box. Decide intentionally.

playground.intro: A single Box you can paint, round, outline, and clip. Toggle clip off to watch the child square spill past the rounded corners — the classic reason a background alone isn't enough.

title: Drawing & graphics: Canvas, DrawScope, Brush and layers
summary: Compose drawing is phase-aware. Use background and Brush for simple fills, drawBehind for decoration behind content, drawWithContent when draw order matters, drawWithCache when expensive drawing objects should be reused, Canvas for standalone custom art, and graphicsLayer for transforms that affect only drawing.
explanation_html:
Compose has a precise graphics ladder. For simple surfaces, prefer background, border, clip, Material Surface, Card, and Brush. When you need custom pixels, use a draw modifier. When the custom pixels are the whole composable, use Canvas. When you need transforms, alpha, clipping, shadow, or offscreen compositing without changing measured layout size, use graphicsLayer.

The core drawing context is DrawScope. Inside Canvas, drawBehind, drawWithContent, and drawWithCache, this is a scoped drawing environment with size, density, layout direction, and draw functions such as drawRect, drawCircle, drawLine, drawPath, and drawRoundRect. Draw calls use pixels, so convert dp values with toPx() inside the draw scope when a shape needs a physical radius or stroke width.

drawBehind is the simple workhorse: it draws behind the composable's normal content. Use it for indicators, custom backgrounds, dividers, chart marks behind labels, or decoration whose order is always behind the content. Canvas is essentially a composable wrapper around drawing; it participates in layout like any other composable and uses the DrawScope for its pixels.

drawWithContent is for explicit draw order. It lets you draw before content, call drawContent(), then draw after content for masks, overlays, reveals, and effects. The footgun is intentional: if you never call drawContent(), the composable's normal content is not rendered. That is useful when replacing content, but it is a bug when you only meant to add an overlay.

drawWithCache is for expensive draw objects whose lifetime should follow drawing size and state reads. Use it when creating a Brush, Path, shader, text layout, or image-derived object that should be reused across draw calls. Do not use it for one cheap drawRect(Color); the cache block itself has overhead. Use onDrawBehind or onDrawWithContent inside the cache block to define the cached drawing behavior.

graphicsLayer creates a draw layer. It can rotate, scale, translate, clip, change alpha, cast shadow, and control compositing. A layer can be reissued efficiently and its properties can be animated without re-running custom drawing code, but it does not change measurement or placement. A scaled or rotated composable can visually overlap neighbors because its layout bounds stay the same. Prefer the lambda version when layer properties read state, especially for animations.
key_points: Use the smallest graphics tool: background/Brush for fills, drawBehind for behind-content drawing, drawWithContent for draw order, drawWithCache for cached draw objects, Canvas for standalone drawing, graphicsLayer for layer transforms.
DrawScope is the drawing context behind Canvas and draw modifiers; it exposes size, density, layout direction, and draw functions.
DrawScope operations use pixels; convert dp with toPx() inside the draw scope for radii, stroke widths, and offsets.
drawBehind always draws behind normal content.
drawWithContent must call drawContent() unless you intentionally want to replace the composable's content.
drawWithCache is valuable when creating Brush, Path, Shader, image, or text-layout objects that should be cached while size/read state is stable.
graphicsLayer affects drawing and compositing, not measurement or placement; transforms can visually overlap other layout bounds.
Brush describes how pixels are painted and works with background, TextStyle, and DrawScope calls.
gotchas_html:
• Using drawWithContent and forgetting drawContent(). The original composable disappears. That is correct for replacement effects, but usually wrong for overlays.
  • Using drawWithCache for cheap drawing. Cache only when you create expensive objects such as Brush, Path, shader, image, or text layout. Simple color rectangles belong in drawBehind.
  • Expecting graphicsLayer to affect layout. Rotation, scale, alpha, and translation happen in the draw layer. The measured size and hit/layout position remain unchanged, so transformed content can overlap neighbors.
  • Allocating draw objects every frame. Creating a Path or gradient inside a regular draw lambda can churn allocations. Use drawWithCache when the object depends only on size or stable state.
  • Forgetting pixels in DrawScope. Draw calls are pixel-based. Use 8.dp.toPx() inside the draw scope instead of hard-coded pixel constants.
  • Drawing over content by accident. drawWithContent order is literal. Drawing after drawContent() overlays the content; drawing before it sits behind.
  • Using Canvas for normal layout decoration. If the drawing belongs behind an existing composable, drawBehind is usually simpler and keeps the semantics/content node intact.

playground.intro: Use the decoration playground as the baseline for when a draw modifier is not needed. If all you need is fill, border, rounding, or clipping, use modifiers first; reach for drawBehind, Canvas, or graphicsLayer when pixels or transforms are genuinely custom.

title: Images & resources: Image, painterResource, AsyncImage and localized assets
summary: Compose resource work is more than drawing a bitmap. Production UI reads localized strings, plurals, dimensions, vector drawables, raster assets, remote images, placeholders, content descriptions, and contentScale deliberately so visuals stay accessible, efficient, and configuration-aware.
explanation_html:
Compose can read Android resources directly from composition. Use stringResource for localized text, pluralStringResource for count-aware strings, dimensionResource when a legacy resource value is still the source of truth, and painterResource for drawable resources. This keeps user-facing text and assets tied to locale, density, night mode, and resource qualifiers instead of hard-coded literals in composables.

Image is the leaf composable for drawing a Painter, ImageBitmap, or ImageVector. For static drawable resources, painterResource(R.drawable.hero) is the common path and works with vector drawables and common raster asset formats. Use Icon for small symbolic imagery that should inherit icon sizing and tint conventions; use Image for photographs, illustrations, and content whose source colors usually matter.

Remote images should use an image loading library rather than manual bitmap work. Coil's AsyncImage composes naturally with Compose, handles background loading, memory and disk caches, placeholders, errors, and request sizing. The sizing part is not optional: give remote images real constraints with Modifier.size, fillMaxWidth plus aspectRatio, height, or sizeIn. Without constraints a loader may have to fetch or decode the original full-size bitmap, which is expensive for feeds and grids.

contentScale decides how source pixels map into destination bounds. ContentScale.Fit preserves the whole image and may letterbox. Crop fills the destination and trims overflow, which is common for avatars and cards. FillBounds stretches to fit and can distort. Inside keeps small images at natural size and scales larger ones down. Pick the policy by product intent, not by trial and error.

Accessibility is part of the image API. A meaningful image needs a concise contentDescription, usually from stringResource. A decorative image that repeats adjacent text should use contentDescription = null so screen readers skip noise. Do not hide avatars, product photos, QR codes, charts, receipts, or state icons unless another semantic label already communicates the same information.

For advanced drawing, create a custom Painter only when resource, vector, bitmap, draw modifiers, or Canvas are not enough. Keep reusable composable APIs stable: pass a drawable resource id, URL/model, ImageVector, or domain image object, then create or remember the Painter inside the composable. Passing arbitrary Painter objects through every API makes recomposition and ownership harder to reason about.
key_points: Use stringResource and pluralStringResource for user-facing text instead of hard-coded strings in composables.
Use painterResource for static vector or raster drawable resources that feed Image, Icon, or Modifier.paint.
Use Image for photographs and illustrations; use Icon for symbolic drawables that should follow icon tint and sizing conventions.
Use AsyncImage or another image-loading library for remote images so loading, caching, placeholders, errors, and downsampling are handled off the hot path.
Constrain remote images with size, width, height, fill plus aspectRatio, or sizeIn so the loader can request an appropriate bitmap size.
Choose ContentScale deliberately: Fit preserves all pixels, Crop fills and trims, FillBounds stretches, and Inside only scales down when needed.
Provide a localized contentDescription for meaningful images; use null only when the image is purely decorative or redundant.
Prefer theme colors for UI color roles; reserve colorResource for cases where a resource color is truly the source of truth.
Create custom Painter implementations only for reusable drawing behavior that cannot be expressed with resources, ImageVector, Canvas, or draw modifiers.
Pass stable image inputs such as @DrawableRes ids, URLs, ImageVector, or ImageBitmap through APIs, then create or remember Painter objects inside the composable.
gotchas_html:
• Hard-coding user-facing text. Text("3 items") works in one language and fails in plural rules. Use stringResource and pluralStringResource.
  • Using contentDescription = null for meaningful images. Null means decorative. Avatars, receipts, charts, QR codes, product photos, and status icons need a label unless surrounding semantics already say the same thing.
  • Letting remote images load unconstrained. An AsyncImage with no size, aspect ratio, or bounded fill can request a full-resolution bitmap. Bound the layout before loading.
  • Picking FillBounds because it fills the box. It stretches and can distort photos. Use Crop for filled cards and avatars, Fit when every pixel must remain visible.
  • Tinting photographs. ColorFilter.tint is usually for icons or intentional effects. Product and content photos normally preserve source colors.
  • Passing Painter as a broad component parameter. Painter objects are not the best stable API boundary. Pass a resource id, URL, vector, bitmap, or domain image model and create the painter inside.
  • Decoding large local bitmaps in hot paths. painterResource is convenient for app assets, but large bitmap work still has memory cost. Prefer appropriately sized assets and image libraries for large or dynamic content.
canonical_code:
import androidx.annotation.DrawableRes
import androidx.compose.foundation.Image
import androidx.compose.foundation.layout.Column
import androidx.compose.foundation.layout.aspectRatio
import androidx.compose.foundation.layout.fillMaxWidth
import androidx.compose.foundation.layout.size
import androidx.compose.foundation.shape.CircleShape
import androidx.compose.foundation.shape.RoundedCornerShape
import androidx.compose.material3.Icon
import androidx.compose.material3.MaterialTheme
import androidx.compose.material3.Text
import androidx.compose.runtime.Composable
import androidx.compose.runtime.remember
import androidx.compose.ui.Modifier
import androidx.compose.ui.draw.clip
import androidx.compose.ui.geometry.Size
import androidx.compose.ui.graphics.Color
import androidx.compose.ui.graphics.ColorFilter
import androidx.compose.ui.graphics.ImageBitmap
import androidx.compose.ui.graphics.StrokeCap
import androidx.compose.ui.graphics.drawscope.DrawScope
import androidx.compose.ui.graphics.drawscope.Stroke
import androidx.compose.ui.graphics.painter.Painter
import androidx.compose.ui.layout.ContentScale
import androidx.compose.ui.platform.LocalContext
import androidx.compose.ui.res.painterResource
import androidx.compose.ui.res.pluralStringResource
import androidx.compose.ui.res.stringResource
import androidx.compose.ui.unit.dp
import coil.compose.AsyncImage
import coil.request.ImageRequest

@Composable
fun ResourceHero(@DrawableRes imageRes: Int, modifier: Modifier = Modifier) {
  Image(
    painter = painterResource(imageRes),
    contentDescription = stringResource(R.string.hero_photo_description),
    contentScale = ContentScale.Crop,
    modifier = modifier
      .fillMaxWidth()
      .aspectRatio(16f / 9f)
      .clip(RoundedCornerShape(20.dp))
  )
}

@Composable
fun RemoteAvatar(url: String, modifier: Modifier = Modifier) {
  AsyncImage(
    model = ImageRequest.Builder(LocalContext.current)
      .data(url)
      .crossfade(true)
      .build(),
    placeholder = painterResource(R.drawable.avatar_placeholder),
    error = painterResource(R.drawable.avatar_error),
    contentDescription = stringResource(R.string.profile_photo_description),
    contentScale = ContentScale.Crop,
    modifier = modifier
      .size(96.dp) // gives the loader a bounded request size
      .clip(CircleShape)
  )
}

@Composable
fun CartIcon(count: Int, modifier: Modifier = Modifier) {
  Column(modifier) {
    Icon(
      painter = painterResource(R.drawable.ic_cart),
      contentDescription = stringResource(R.string.cart_icon_description),
      tint = MaterialTheme.colorScheme.primary,
      modifier = Modifier.size(32.dp)
    )
    Text(
      pluralStringResource(
        R.plurals.items_in_cart,
        count,
        count
      )
    )
  }
}

class ProgressPainter(private val color: Color) : Painter() {
  override val intrinsicSize: Size = Size.Unspecified

  override fun DrawScope.onDraw() {
    val stroke = 6.dp.toPx()
    drawCircle(color.copy(alpha = 0.14f), radius = size.minDimension / 2f)
    drawArc(
      color = color,
      startAngle = -90f,
      sweepAngle = 270f,
      useCenter = false,
      style = Stroke(stroke, cap = StrokeCap.Round)
    )
  }
}

@Composable
fun ProgressBadge(modifier: Modifier = Modifier) {
  val color = MaterialTheme.colorScheme.primary
  val painter = remember(color) { ProgressPainter(color) }
  Image(
    painter = painter,
    contentDescription = stringResource(R.string.progress_badge_description),
    contentScale = ContentScale.Fit,
    modifier = modifier.size(72.dp)
  )
}

playground.intro: Switch the image source and contentScale to see which Compose API belongs to each case. Then toggle description, clip, placeholder, and tint to compare accessibility, loading, and visual behavior.

title: Scroll
summary: Add Modifier.verticalScroll / horizontalScroll to let a fixed-size container scroll its overflowing content — but only for small, known-size content, since every child is composed eagerly.
explanation_html:
By default a Compose layout is exactly as tall (or wide) as its content, and a Column/Row placed in a constrained parent simply clips anything that overflows — there is no scrolling for free. To make content scrollable you attach a scroll modifier: Modifier.verticalScroll(scrollState) for up/down or Modifier.horizontalScroll(scrollState) for left/right. The modifier reads the available space from the parent, lets its content measure itself with unbounded space in the scroll direction, and then only shows the slice that fits — translating the rest in and out of view as the user drags.

The single required argument is a ScrollState, which you almost always create with val scrollState = rememberScrollState(). remember is essential: it survives recomposition so the scroll position is not reset on every frame, and the value is automatically saved/restored across configuration changes (it has a Saver). The ScrollState exposes scrollState.value (current offset in pixels, an Int), scrollState.maxValue (the maximum offset), plus suspend functions scrollTo(value) and animateScrollTo(value) that you call from a CoroutineScope to programmatically jump or smoothly scroll. The other optional parameters of the modifier are enabled (default true), flingBehavior (the momentum/deceleration after a fling), and reverseScrolling (default false); reversing flips both the gesture direction and the start position so content begins scrolled to the end.

The defining trade-off of these modifiers is eager composition. A scroll modifier does not virtualize: it composes, measures, and lays out every child up front, then just clips and offsets them. That is perfectly fine for a settings screen, a long article, or a form with a dozen fields — content whose size is bounded and known. It is the wrong tool for a feed of hundreds or thousands of items, because building all of them eagerly wastes memory and stalls the first frame. For long or unbounded lists you switch to LazyColumn / LazyRow, which only compose the items currently visible (plus a small buffer) and recycle them as you scroll. A useful rule of thumb: verticalScroll on a Column = small/finite content; LazyColumn = long/dynamic content.

Order and constraints matter. The scroll modifier must sit on the element whose own size is constrained while its content can grow — typically the Column itself: Column(Modifier.verticalScroll(state)). Because the content is measured with infinite space along the scroll axis, you cannot use Modifier.weight or fillMaxHeight() on children inside a vertically scrolling Column — there is no finite height to take a fraction of, and Compose will throw or behave unexpectedly. The scrolling container itself, however, should usually be height-constrained (for example by fillMaxSize(), a fixed height, or a parent that bounds it); if nothing constrains it, it will just grow to fit all content and never need to scroll.

Two related but distinct APIs round out the picture. Modifier.scrollable(state, orientation) is the low-level primitive: it detects scroll gestures and reports the delta to a ScrollableState, but it does not move or clip any content for you — you decide what to do with the delta. verticalScroll/horizontalScroll are the batteries-included version built on top of it that actually offset and clip the content. You generally reach for scrollable() only when building custom scroll-driven effects (parallax, collapsing headers), and use the dedicated verticalScroll/horizontalScroll for ordinary content scrolling.
key_points: Modifier.verticalScroll / horizontalScroll make a fixed-size container scroll its overflowing content; without them, overflow is simply clipped.
Always pass a remembered ScrollState: val state = rememberScrollState() — remember keeps the offset stable across recomposition and restores it across config changes.
Content is composed EAGERLY: every child is built, measured, and laid out up front. Use it only for small/known-size content.
For long or unbounded lists use LazyColumn / LazyRow instead, which virtualize (compose only visible items).
scrollState.value and scrollState.maxValue are in PIXELS (Int); scrollTo/animateScrollTo are suspend functions called from a CoroutineScope.
Inside a vertically scrolling Column you cannot use Modifier.weight or fillMaxHeight on children — the scroll axis is measured as unbounded.
Optional params: enabled (default true), reverseScrolling (default false, flips direction + start), and flingBehavior for momentum.
Modifier.scrollable(state, orientation) only reports gesture deltas — it does not offset or clip content; verticalScroll/horizontalScroll are built on top of it and do.
The scrolling container should itself be size-constrained (fillMaxSize, fixed height, or a bounding parent); otherwise it grows to fit and never scrolls.
gotchas_html:
The most common scroll mistakes come from forgetting why these modifiers exist and how they measure:

  • Calling ScrollState(0) or rememberScrollState() without remember. A bare ScrollState(0) is re-created on every recomposition, so the position jumps back to the top constantly. Always use rememberScrollState().
  • Using verticalScroll for a huge list. Because composition is eager, a Column(Modifier.verticalScroll(...)) with hundreds of items composes all of them at once — janky first frame and high memory. Reach for LazyColumn when the count is large or unknown.
  • Putting weight() or fillMaxHeight() on children of a vertically scrolling Column. The scroll axis is measured as infinite, so there is no finite height to divide — this throws IllegalStateException or collapses children to zero. Give children intrinsic or fixed sizes instead.
  • Nesting two scrollables on the same axis (e.g. a verticalScroll Column inside a LazyColumn) causes the inner one to be measured with infinite height and breaks layout. Hoist content into a single scroller, or use the host list's own item slots.
  • Expecting the container to scroll when nothing constrains its size. If the scrolling element can grow freely (no fillMaxSize, fixed height, or bounding parent), it simply expands to fit all content and there is nothing to scroll.
  • Treating scrollState.value as dp. It is in raw pixels; convert with the current Density if you need dp, and remember scrollTo takes pixels too.
  • Adding both verticalScroll and horizontalScroll on the same axis story — they are independent axes, but stacking the wrong one (e.g. horizontalScroll on a Column whose content does not overflow horizontally) does nothing useful.

playground.intro: Toggle scrolling on a Column packed with more children than fit. With scroll off and a short height, overflow is clipped; turn scroll on to drag through it. Shrink the height to force overflow.

title: Lazy lists: LazyColumn / LazyRow
summary: LazyColumn and LazyRow render only the items currently visible (plus a small buffer), making them the right tool for long or unbounded scrolling lists where a verticalScroll Column would compose everything at once.
explanation_html:
A plain Column with Modifier.verticalScroll() composes, measures, and lays out every child immediately, even the ones off-screen. That is fine for a handful of items but catastrophic for hundreds or an unknown number — you pay memory and CPU for content nobody sees. LazyColumn (vertical) and LazyRow (horizontal) solve this by only composing the items in (or near) the viewport and recycling them as you scroll. They are Compose's equivalent of RecyclerView, but you describe content declaratively instead of writing an adapter.

The crucial difference is the content block. A Column takes ordinary @Composable content, so you write children directly. A LazyColumn takes a LazyListScope lambda — a DSL, not regular composable content. Inside it you call item { } for a single element, or items(count) / items(list) / itemsIndexed(list) to emit many. You cannot just drop a loop of composables in the body; you declare items through these functions so the framework knows the item boundaries and can compose them individually on demand. The most common form is items(myList) { element -> Row { ... } }, where the trailing lambda runs once per visible element.

Spacing between items is set with verticalArrangement = Arrangement.spacedBy(8.dp) on LazyColumn (or horizontalArrangement on LazyRow) — do not put a Spacer after each item, since that adds a trailing gap and confuses item sizing. For padding, prefer contentPadding = PaddingValues(16.dp) over Modifier.padding(). contentPadding insets the content, so the first and last items can scroll fully into the padded edge region and the padding still shows at the scroll extremes; a Modifier.padding() instead clips the scrollable viewport, hiding content under the padding as it scrolls. This matters most for top/bottom (or start/end) padding on scrollable lists.

Because items are composed and disposed as they scroll, Compose needs a stable identity for each item to preserve state, animate reorders, and keep scroll position correct when the list changes. Provide it with the key parameter: items(users, key = { it.id }) { ... }. Without keys, Compose falls back to item position, so inserting or removing an item can make state (like a remembered expanded flag or a text field's contents) jump to the wrong row. Keys must be unique and, ideally, stable and savable (primitives, strings, or types Compose can store in a Bundle). The optional contentType parameter lets you tell Compose which items share a layout so it can reuse compositions more efficiently across types.

Two structural rules keep lazy lists healthy. First, never nest a scrollable in the same direction: a LazyColumn inside a vertically scrolling Column, or a LazyColumn given a fixed-but-then-wrapped height, throws "Vertically scrollable component was measured with an infinity maximum height constraints" — the parent offers unbounded height and the lazy list has nothing to measure against. Instead, fold the outer content into the same list using item { Header() } blocks, or give the lazy list a bounded size. Second, do not set a lazy list's main-axis size by wrapping content you intended to scroll — let it fill or take a bounded height (Modifier.fillMaxSize(), Modifier.weight(1f), or a fixed height). With Modifier.height(Dp.Unspecified)-style wrapping there is nothing to scroll within.
key_points: LazyColumn/LazyRow only compose items in or near the viewport; a verticalScroll Column composes all children eagerly — use lazy lists for long or unbounded data.
Content goes in a LazyListScope DSL: item { } for one, items(count)/items(list)/itemsIndexed(list) for many — not regular composable children or a for-loop of composables.
Set inter-item spacing with verticalArrangement = Arrangement.spacedBy(8.dp) (LazyColumn) or horizontalArrangement (LazyRow), not trailing Spacers.
Use contentPadding = PaddingValues(...) so edge padding scrolls with the content; Modifier.padding() clips the viewport and hides scrolling content under the padding.
Provide a stable, unique key (items(list, key = { it.id })) so item state, animations, and scroll position survive insertions, removals, and reorders.
Without keys, items are identified by position, so state can attach to the wrong row after the list changes.
Never nest a same-direction scrollable (LazyColumn inside verticalScroll Column) — it causes an 'infinity maximum height' crash; merge content into one list with item { } blocks instead.
Give the lazy list a bounded main-axis size (fillMaxSize, weight(1f), or fixed height); wrapping its scroll content gives it nothing to scroll.
contentType on items lets Compose reuse compositions efficiently when a list mixes several item layouts.
gotchas_html:
The most common lazy-list mistakes:

  • Nesting a LazyColumn in a verticalScroll Column. This throws "Vertically scrollable component was measured with an infinity maximum height constraints". Don't wrap a lazy list in a same-direction scroll. Move headers/footers into the list via item { }, or give the inner list a fixed height.
  • Using Modifier.padding() instead of contentPadding. With Modifier.padding(top = 16.dp) the top item scrolls up and vanishes under the padding; with contentPadding = PaddingValues(top = 16.dp) the padding scrolls away with the content and the first item can reach the edge.
