The single most useful habit is to read the indentation as depth. Compose code is laid out so that the brace nesting mirrors the on-screen hierarchy one-to-one. If a Button is two indents deeper than the Card, it is two levels down the tree and visually sits inside that card. This is why formatting matters: the shape of the code is the shape of the UI.

Layout in Compose runs as a single tree pass and it is worth knowing the direction of travel:

  • Constraints flow DOWN. A parent measures each child by handing it constraints (a min/max width and height). Modifier.fillMaxWidth(), for example, just tells the child to take the maximum width its parent offered.
  • Sizes flow UP. Each child measures itself within those constraints and reports its chosen size back to the parent. The parent then uses those sizes to place its children and to decide its own size.
  • Because of this, a child can never be bigger than the constraints its parent gives it — a Box(Modifier.size(500.dp)) inside a small parent will still be clipped to what the parent allows.

As a tree grows past a screenful of code, you extract nested chunks into their own @Composable functions. A repeated profile row becomes @Composable fun ProfileRow(...); the screen then just calls ProfileRow() three times. This is exactly how the teaching projects' NoteCard and ComponentSection work — extraction does not change the tree, it just gives a subtree a name so you can reuse it and read the parent at a higher altitude.

Finally, the same { } content-slot mechanism is everywhere, not just in layout containers: Button { Text("Save") }, Card { ... } and Scaffold { innerPadding -> ... } all take their children through a trailing @Composable () -> Unit lambda. Once you see nesting as "filling a content slot", the whole framework looks consistent.
key_points: A Compose UI is a single tree; you build it by nesting composables inside one another.
Container composables (Column, Row, Box, Card, Surface, Scaffold) expose their children through a trailing { } content slot — a @Composable () -> Unit lambda.
Leaf composables (Text, Icon, Image, Spacer) have no { } slot because they contain nothing.
Indentation/brace nesting in the code mirrors the visual hierarchy one-to-one — read indentation as depth.
During layout, constraints (min/max width & height) flow DOWN from parent to child.
Children measure themselves within those constraints and report their chosen sizes UP to the parent, which then places them.
A child can't exceed the constraints its parent gives it, so size and fill modifiers are always relative to the parent.
Extract a repeated or deep subtree into its own @Composable function to name and reuse it — the same idea as Button { } and Card { } slots.
gotchas_html:
• Forgetting children get measured by the parent's constraints. Modifier.fillMaxSize() on a child fills the parent's available space, not the whole screen. If the parent is a wrap-content Column, "fill" has nothing to fill and the child collapses. Make the parent give real space first (e.g. the root Column/Box uses fillMaxSize()).
  • Putting children in the parentheses instead of the trailing lambda. Children belong in the { } content slot, not as positional arguments: it's Column(modifier = ...) { Text("hi") }, never Column(Text("hi")). The first positional argument of a layout is its modifier, not its content.
  • Nesting endless scrollers. You cannot put a verticalScroll Column (or a LazyColumn) directly inside another vertically-scrolling parent of the same axis — the child is handed an unbounded height constraint and Compose throws an "infinite/unbounded height" measurement error. Give the inner one a fixed height, or restructure so only one owns the scroll.
  • Treating Box like Column/Row. A Box stacks its children on top of each other (z-order), it does not lay them out in a line. Each child positions itself with Modifier.align(...); reach for Column/Row when you want them placed in sequence.
  • Over-nesting wrapper layouts. A Box or Column that wraps a single child for no layout reason just adds a useless tree node. Apply the modifier to the child directly instead of swaddling it.
  • Letting one function grow the whole tree. A 300-line composable is hard to read and recomposes as one unit. Extract subtrees into named @Composable functions — it both reads better and scopes recomposition.

playground.intro: Drag nestDepth to wrap the same content in progressively more containers — Card › Column › Row › … — and watch the indentation in the generated code grow in lockstep with the tree on screen. Each level is one more { } content slot. Read the indentation as depth: the deeper a composable sits, the deeper it lives in the UI tree.

title: Whole screen: Scaffold & insets
summary: Scaffold lays out the standard app skeleton — top bar, bottom bar, FAB, and content — and hands your content lambda the innerPadding you must apply so nothing hides behind the bars or system insets.
explanation_html:
Scaffold is Material 3's standard screen skeleton. You hand it slots — topBar (usually a TopAppBar), bottomBar (a BottomAppBar or NavigationBar), and floatingActionButton — and a trailing content lambda. Scaffold measures whatever bars you gave it, then lays your content out in the leftover space so the structural pieces never overlap.

The single most important detail: the content lambda receives a PaddingValues (conventionally named innerPadding), and you must apply it with Modifier.padding(innerPadding). This padding is how Scaffold tells your content "leave this much room so you clear the top bar, the bottom bar, and the system bars." Ignore it and your first list item slides under the TopAppBar while the last one hides behind the navigation bar. A common refinement for scrollable content is to pass innerPadding as contentPadding to a LazyColumn instead of as an outer Modifier.padding, so rows can scroll edge-to-edge while still starting and ending in the clear.

Edge-to-edge and insets. Since Android 15 (targetSdk 35) apps draw edge-to-edge by default, and you opt in explicitly by calling enableEdgeToEdge() in onCreate() before setContent. That means your UI paints behind the translucent status and navigation bars. Without any inset handling, content collides with the clock, the camera cutout, and the gesture pill. Scaffold consumes the system-bar insets for you and folds them into innerPadding — so the same padding that clears your bars also clears the system bars. If you place a bar, Scaffold even extends that bar's background up into the status-bar inset automatically.

Without a Scaffold you handle insets manually with inset modifiers. Modifier.systemBarsPadding() pads for the status and navigation bars; Modifier.safeDrawingPadding() is the safest choice because it also accounts for the display cutout and the IME (keyboard). These read from WindowInsets (e.g. WindowInsets.systemBars, WindowInsets.safeDrawing). The classic edge-to-edge bug — text tucked under the status bar or a button under the gesture pill — comes from a raw setContent { /* root with no inset padding */ } after enableEdgeToEdge(). Use Scaffold's innerPadding, or one of these modifiers, to fix it.
key_points: Scaffold provides slots: topBar, bottomBar, floatingActionButton, snackbarHost, plus a trailing content lambda for the body.
The content lambda receives a PaddingValues — conventionally innerPadding — which you MUST apply via Modifier.padding(innerPadding).
innerPadding already includes both the space taken by your bars AND the system-bar insets, so applying it clears everything at once.
Call enableEdgeToEdge() in onCreate() before setContent to draw behind the status and navigation bars; on Android 15+ (targetSdk 35) edge-to-edge is the default.
For scrollable content, pass innerPadding as a LazyColumn's contentPadding so rows scroll under the bars but still start/end clear, instead of clipping with outer padding.
Without a Scaffold, use Modifier.systemBarsPadding() for status+nav bars, or Modifier.safeDrawingPadding() which also covers the display cutout and IME.
WindowInsets exposes the raw values (WindowInsets.systemBars, WindowInsets.safeDrawing) that those modifiers consume.
Scaffold automatically draws a placed bar's background up into the status-bar inset, so the top bar reaches the screen edge without manual work.
gotchas_html:
• Ignoring innerPadding. Writing Scaffold(topBar = { TopAppBar(...) }) { Column { ... } } and never touching the lambda parameter is the #1 mistake — your content renders under the top bar. Apply Modifier.padding(innerPadding) to the root of your content.
  • Applying innerPadding in the wrong place for lists. Putting Modifier.padding(innerPadding) on a LazyColumn clips the scroll area so rows can't reach the edges. Prefer LazyColumn(contentPadding = innerPadding) so items scroll edge-to-edge yet still stop short of the bars.
  • Raw setContent after enableEdgeToEdge() with no inset handling. Your title slides under the clock and a bottom button hides behind the gesture pill. Wrap the root in a Scaffold, or add Modifier.safeDrawingPadding().
  • Double padding. Applying systemBarsPadding() inside a Scaffold's content (which already consumed those insets via innerPadding) double-counts the insets and leaves an ugly gap. Inside a Scaffold, use innerPadding only.
  • Forgetting the cutout/IME. systemBarsPadding() does not cover the display cutout or keyboard. If content must clear those too, use safeDrawingPadding().
  • Using the wrong TopAppBar overload. TopAppBar with a scroll behavior needs you to pass scrollBehavior and wire Modifier.nestedScroll(scrollBehavior.nestedScrollConnection) onto the Scaffold; omitting it means the bar won't collapse on scroll.

playground.intro: Toggle the bars and FAB on, then flip the insets control through none → systemBarsPadding → Scaffold to watch where the content lands. With 'none' the content tucks under the bars (the classic edge-to-edge bug); 'systemBarsPadding' clears the system bars manually; 'Scaffold' lets innerPadding clear both the bars and the system insets for you.

title: Edge-to-edge & WindowInsets: system bars, cutouts and IME
summary: Edge-to-edge is now a production requirement, not a visual flourish. Compose screens must explicitly protect important content from status bars, navigation bars, caption bars, display cutouts, system gestures, and the software keyboard while still drawing into the full window.
explanation_html:
Edge-to-edge means the app can draw behind the system bars: status bar, navigation bar, and caption bar in desktop-windowing modes. On Android 15 and higher, edge-to-edge is enforced when the app targets SDK 35, so older assumptions that content starts below system bars are no longer safe. The Activity setup matters: call enableEdgeToEdge() in onCreate(), and use android:windowSoftInputMode="adjustResize" for screens that need software-keyboard insets.

WindowInsets tells Compose where system UI and system gestures occupy the window. The important families are statusBars, navigationBars, captionBar, systemBars, displayCutout, ime, systemGestures, and the safe groups: safeDrawing, safeGestures, and safeContent. Use safeDrawing for content that must not be visually obscured, safeGestures when app gestures would conflict with system gestures, and safeContent when both are true.

There are three main tools. Padding modifiers such as safeDrawingPadding(), systemBarsPadding(), navigationBarsPadding(), and imePadding() apply insets as padding. Size modifiers such as windowInsetsBottomHeight(WindowInsets.systemBars) size an element, usually a Spacer, to the inset. WindowInsets values and rulers are lower-level tools for custom layouts. Prefer the built-in padding and size modifiers because they read inset values in the layout phase, on the same frame that the platform updates them.

Inset consumption prevents double padding. Padding modifiers consume the part of the inset they apply, so nested inset-aware content knows what is already handled. When a parent uses ordinary padding or Scaffold innerPadding, use consumeWindowInsets(innerPadding) before children add their own inset-aware spacers or IME padding. If you stack innerPadding, safeDrawingPadding(), and navigationBarsPadding() on the same edge without a consumption boundary, the screen will grow mysterious extra gaps.

Material 3 helps, but it does not remove responsibility. Many Material 3 bars and sheets handle the insets that belong to their placement. Scaffold provides innerPadding for content to use, but it does not apply that padding to your content automatically. A common pattern is LazyColumn(modifier = Modifier.consumeWindowInsets(innerPadding), contentPadding = innerPadding), then explicit content padding and inset spacers where needed. Avoid adding more inset modifiers on top of Scaffold unless you know which edge each owner handles.

IME behavior is a special case because it animates. Compose inset types animate automatically, with IME animation support backported to API 21. For scrolling forms and chats, apply imePadding() to the scrolling container, add imeNestedScroll() when you want scrolling to drive keyboard movement, and use a bottom Spacer(Modifier.windowInsetsBottomHeight(WindowInsets.systemBars)) for lazy lists rather than relying only on bottom contentPadding. Test with gesture navigation, three-button navigation, landscape, cutouts, freeform caption bars, and the keyboard opening on the last field.
key_points: Android 15 enforces edge-to-edge for apps targeting SDK 35, so content can draw behind system bars and cutouts by default.
Call enableEdgeToEdge() in Activity.onCreate() and set windowSoftInputMode="adjustResize" when screens need IME insets.
WindowInsets describes status bars, navigation bars, caption bars, display cutouts, IME, tappable areas, and system gestures.
Use safeDrawing for visual protection, safeGestures for gesture conflicts, and safeContent when both matter.
Prefer inset padding and size modifiers because they apply inset values during layout, not stale composition reads.
Scaffold provides innerPadding for you to apply; it does not automatically inset the screen content.
Material 3 bars, rails, navigation bars, drawers, and sheets often handle their own placement insets.
Use consumeWindowInsets when parent padding has already accounted for insets and descendants still need inset-aware behavior.
Use imePadding and imeNestedScroll for keyboard-aware scroll containers; Compose animates inset changes with the IME.
For lazy lists, add an inset-sized bottom Spacer so the last item can clear system bars and IME transitions.
gotchas_html:
• Assuming old non-edge-to-edge defaults. Android 15 target SDK 35 changes the baseline: content may be behind system bars unless you handle insets.
  • Forgetting Activity setup. Compose modifiers cannot receive correct keyboard space if the Activity never enables edge-to-edge or omits adjustResize for IME-driven screens.
  • Ignoring Scaffold innerPadding. The padding is passed to you; content still hides behind bars if you never apply it.
  • Double applying the same edge. Modifier.padding(innerPadding).safeDrawingPadding().navigationBarsPadding() is usually a double-padding bug unless consumption is deliberate.
  • Using constants as fake system bars. A hardcoded 24.dp or 48.dp does not track gesture navigation, three-button navigation, taskbars, cutouts, caption bars, landscape, or IME animation.
  • Reading raw inset values in composition for layout. Insets update after composition and before layout. Prefer the built-in inset modifiers for layout-affecting work.
  • Only testing one navigation mode. Gesture navigation, three-button navigation, tablets, foldables, freeform windows, cutouts, and the keyboard expose different inset shapes.

playground.intro: Use Scaffold as the edge-to-edge shell: top and bottom bars can own their Material insets, content must apply innerPadding, and keyboard-heavy screens need deliberate IME and system-bar handling.

title: Interop & migration: ComposeView, AndroidView and existing apps
summary: Most production Compose work starts inside an existing Android app. Interop is the discipline of choosing the right boundary between Views and composables, disposing compositions with the host lifecycle, preserving one source of truth, and replacing legacy UI one seam at a time.
explanation_html:
Interop is not a compromise layer to ignore after the demo works. It is the production contract between an existing View or Fragment app and new Compose UI. The main question is which side owns the screen at this point in the migration: a View host containing a ComposeView, a Compose screen containing one legacy AndroidView, a temporary AndroidFragment, or a shared AbstractComposeView wrapper used from XML or View code.

For Compose inside Views, ComposeView creates a composition in the View tree. In Fragment onCreateView, set ViewCompositionStrategy.DisposeOnViewTreeLifecycleDestroyed so the composition is disposed with the Fragment view lifecycle, not left to a detached-window heuristic. Activity and pooling-container cases can use different defaults, but Fragment views are recreated often enough that the lifecycle owner must be explicit.

For Views inside Compose, use AndroidView only when there is no Compose equivalent or the migration intentionally keeps a proven View component. Create the View inside the factory lambda, push Compose state into it through update, and clean transient state through onReset when the View appears in lazy lists, grids, or pagers. AndroidViewBinding is useful for small legacy XML islands; it should not become a way to keep whole screen layouts inside a Compose-only destination.

Fragments inside Compose are a transitionary step. AndroidFragment removes the hosted Fragment when the composable leaves composition, but a Compose-first app should move toward a single Activity with Compose navigation rather than keep Fragment boundaries forever. Similarly, AbstractComposeView is best for a small shared component exposed to View screens while the app builds a Compose component library.

The migration usually succeeds or fails on state ownership. Split state from presentation before rewriting the UI: legacy Views, adapters, and fragments should stop being the source of truth for business state. Compose should receive immutable state and events, while View wrappers expose the smallest mutable inputs required by the boundary. When nested scroll crosses the View/Compose boundary, bridge it deliberately with rememberNestedScrollInteropConnection() only for cooperating View parents.
key_points: Migrate incrementally: new Compose screens, shared Compose components, and one legacy feature slice at a time.
Use ComposeView when an existing View or Fragment screen needs to host composable content.
Fragment-hosted ComposeView content should set ViewCompositionStrategy.DisposeOnViewTreeLifecycleDestroyed.
Use AndroidView only for legacy View components without a suitable Compose replacement.
Create the View inside AndroidView factory and drive it from Compose state through update.
Use AndroidView onReset when the View is placed in lazy lists, grids, pagers, or other reusable item content.
Use AndroidViewBinding for small legacy XML islands, not full Compose-only screen layouts.
Use AndroidFragment only as a transitionary bridge for existing Fragments during migration.
Expose shared Compose UI to View code with AbstractComposeView when a small reusable component must work on both sides.
Move state ownership out of legacy Views before replacing UI so Compose remains state down, events up.
gotchas_html:
• Fragment ComposeView without a lifecycle strategy. A Fragment view can be destroyed and recreated while the Fragment instance survives. Use DisposeOnViewTreeLifecycleDestroyed for content returned from onCreateView.
  • Remembering a View outside AndroidView. Construct the View in factory; Compose controls creation, update, reset, and release through that API.
  • Lazy AndroidView without reset. Reused View instances can leak selected rows, listeners, scroll position, player state, or old data unless onReset clears transient state.
  • Keeping adapters as the source of truth. RecyclerView and ViewHolder migration works best after item state and events are already represented as plain models.
  • Inflating whole screens with AndroidViewBinding. Binding a small XML widget is fine during migration. Keeping a full XML screen inside a Compose route blocks Compose testing, theming, and navigation benefits.
  • Treating AndroidFragment as final architecture. It is a bridge for existing fragments, not a reason to keep Fragment boundaries in a Compose-only app.
  • Assuming nested scroll always crosses the boundary. Use rememberNestedScrollInteropConnection() for cooperating View parents; non-cooperating parents such as some RecyclerView or ViewPager2 setups need different migration choices.

playground.intro: Choose the migration boundary and watch the generated Kotlin change. The goal is not to mix APIs everywhere; it is to keep one deliberate seam with lifecycle, state ownership, reuse, and nested scroll handled explicitly.

title: Navigation Compose: graphs, routes & back stack
summary: Navigation Compose connects app screens through a graph, displays the current destination in a NavHost, coordinates movement with NavController, and now supports type-safe serializable routes for safer arguments.
explanation_html:
Navigation is the contract for moving across, into, and back out of app content. In a Compose-first app, the navigation host is usually NavHost: it owns the visible destination and swaps composable destinations as the back stack changes. The NavController is the coordinator that navigates, pops, handles deep links, and owns the stack history.

The production pattern is to keep navigation at the route or app shell layer. Leaf UI should receive plain state and callbacks such as onOpenProfile(id), not a NavController. That keeps buttons, rows, and screen content previewable, testable, and reusable. The route layer translates those callbacks into navController.navigate(...), navigateUp(), or a pop operation.

Modern Navigation Compose supports type-safe routes. Instead of fragile strings such as "profile/{id}", define serializable route objects or data classes and use composable<Profile>. Arguments become typed constructor parameters, navigate(Profile(id)) creates the route, and backStackEntry.toRoute<Profile>() reconstructs the argument object. This gives you compile-time pressure when a route shape changes.

Back stack policy is part of the design. Bottom navigation often uses popUpTo, launchSingleTop, and restoreState so tabs do not stack duplicates and each tab can restore its prior state. Detail screens should usually receive stable IDs, then let a ViewModel or repository load the larger data. Avoid passing large objects through routes; route arguments should identify the destination, not carry the whole screen model.
key_points: NavHost is the Compose navigation host: it displays the destination at the top of the current navigation state.
NavController is the coordinator for navigate, navigateUp, popping, deep links, and back-stack state.
Keep NavController at the route/app shell layer; pass plain navigation callbacks into leaf composables.
Prefer type-safe routes with serializable objects or data classes instead of literal route strings.
Use object routes for destinations with no arguments and data classes for destinations with typed arguments.
Read typed arguments with NavBackStackEntry.toRoute<T>() or SavedStateHandle.toRoute<T>() in a ViewModel.
Pass stable IDs or small arguments through routes; load large screen data from a data layer.
For top-level destinations, use nav options such as popUpTo, launchSingleTop, restoreState, and saveState to avoid duplicate stacks.
gotchas_html:
• Passing NavController everywhere. Leaf UI becomes hard to preview and test. Keep navigation in route composables and pass lambdas down.
  • Stringly typed routes. navigate("profile/$id") works until a path or argument name changes. Prefer serializable typed routes.
  • Passing whole models as arguments. Routes should identify data with IDs or small primitives; repositories/ViewModels should load the full model.
  • Duplicating top-level destinations. Bottom navigation that calls navigate(Home) repeatedly without launchSingleTop or popUpTo can pile up repeated copies.
  • Doing navigation from the composable body. Navigate from event callbacks or controlled effects, not as a direct side effect of composition.
  • Forgetting back behavior in tests. A graph is not complete until you have checked destination selection, argument loading, up, and system back behavior.

playground.intro: Use the Scaffold toggles as a shell for a navigation graph: top-level destinations often live behind bars, while detail destinations are pushed onto the back stack and navigate back up. The key idea is that route-level callbacks trigger navigation while the content stays stateless.

title: Navigation 3: owned back stacks, NavDisplay and scenes
summary: Navigation 3 is the Compose-first navigation model: destinations are keys, the app owns the back stack as state, entryProvider resolves keys to content, and NavDisplay renders the current scene. It is the right mental model when navigation must be explicit, saveable, testable, adaptive, and controlled by normal Compose state.
explanation_html:
Navigation 3 changes the shape of Compose navigation. Instead of sending events into a controller that owns a graph, your app owns a snapshot-state back stack. Navigating forward means adding a key to a list; going back means removing one. NavDisplay observes that state and renders the matching destination content through an entryProvider.

The core abstraction is a key, usually a small serializable object or data class. A key identifies content; it should not contain loaded screen models, large payloads, repositories, or UI objects. When the back stack must survive configuration changes and process death, use keys that implement NavKey and are annotated with @Serializable, then create the stack with rememberNavBackStack.

entryProvider is the destination map. It resolves each key type to a NavEntry and composable content. This keeps the state model and UI wiring explicit: leaf content still receives plain state plus callbacks, while the route or app shell mutates the back stack in response to those callbacks. Testing becomes simpler because navigation state is just data you can inspect and mutate.

Navigation 3 also makes state scoping and adaptive presentation explicit. entryDecorators can retain saveable state and scope ViewModels to individual back-stack entries. metadata lets entries communicate display preferences such as transitions, dialog behavior, or scene hints to containers. Scene and SceneStrategy let NavDisplay render more than one entry at once, which is the foundation for adaptive list-detail or multi-pane flows.

Migration from Navigation Compose should be deliberate. Update route types to NavKey, create an owned navigation state holder, move graph destinations into entryProvider, replace NavHost with NavDisplay, wire onBack, then remove old Navigation 2 dependencies only after behavior, saved state, dialogs, transitions, and tests are covered.
key_points: Navigation 3 is designed for Compose and gives the app full control over the back stack.
The back stack is modeled as observable state: pushing and popping keys changes what NavDisplay renders.
Keys identify destinations; keep them small and use IDs instead of loaded screen objects.
Use NavKey plus @Serializable when a Navigation 3 back stack must be saved and restored.
rememberNavBackStack creates a saveable back stack for serializable NavKey entries.
entryProvider maps key types to NavEntry content and keeps destination wiring explicit.
NavDisplay observes the back stack and should receive an onBack handler that mutates the same owned stack.
entryDecorators retain saveable state and can scope ViewModels to individual NavEntry instances.
metadata lets entries communicate transitions, dialog behavior, or scene intent to NavDisplay and SceneStrategies.
Scenes can render multiple NavEntry instances, enabling adaptive multi-pane navigation from the same back stack.
gotchas_html:
• Treating Navigation 3 like NavController with a new name. The state model is different. Navigation happens by mutating owned back-stack state.
  • Using unsaveable keys. If keys are not @Serializable NavKey values, rememberNavBackStack cannot preserve the stack through process death.
  • Putting screen models in keys. Keys should identify destinations. Load full data from repositories or ViewModels from the destination boundary.
  • Leaving NavDisplay without onBack. Back behavior should remove a key or delegate to the same navigation state holder.
  • Mixing NavHost and NavDisplay in one flow. Half-migration creates two back-stack authorities. Migrate a feature atomically.
  • Forgetting entry decorators. Destination saveable state and ViewModel lifetime need the right decorators when they should be scoped to entries, not the whole Activity.
  • Using metadata as global state. Metadata is for display/layout hints consumed by Navigation 3 components, not for business data or screen source of truth.

playground.intro: Switch between Navigation 3 concerns. The visual stack is owned state; NavDisplay observes it and renders entries. Saveable keys, entry decorators, and metadata are deliberate additions, not hidden controller behavior.

title: Adaptive layouts: window size classes & panes
summary: Adaptive Compose UI bases major layout decisions on the app window, not the physical device. Use window size classes, pane scaffolds, and adaptive navigation so one feature works on phones, tablets, foldables, ChromeOS, desktop windows, and split screen.
explanation_html:
Adaptive UI starts with a simple rule: decide from the current app window, not the full physical display. A tablet app in split screen might have a compact window; a foldable might have a hinge or posture that changes the useful area; a ChromeOS or desktop-windowing app can be resized continuously. Compose is a good fit because layout is a function of state, so changing window state can produce a different screen structure without separate XML layout files.

The primary signal is the window size class. Use currentWindowAdaptiveInfo().windowSizeClass from Compose Material 3 Adaptive at the app shell or route level, derive a small configuration such as one-pane versus two-pane, and pass that configuration down like any other state. Avoid making leaf cards and rows query global display size independently; it scatters layout policy across the app and makes previews harder.

For large windows, think in panes. A phone often shows one pane at a time: list, then detail. A tablet or expanded window can show the list and detail side by side. Material 3 Adaptive provides pane scaffolds such as NavigableListDetailPaneScaffold that combine layout, pane navigation, and back behavior so the same feature can move between one-pane and multi-pane presentations.

Adaptive does not mean sprinkling breakpoints everywhere. It means putting the screen-level decision in one place, keeping inner components flexible, and testing resize paths: compact, medium, expanded, split screen, rotation, font scale, and fold posture where relevant. The nested composables should still be good Compose citizens: modifier parameter, no fixed phone-only widths, accessible touch targets, stable lazy keys, and clear state ownership.
key_points: Base adaptive decisions on the current app window, not the physical device screen.
Use currentWindowAdaptiveInfo().windowSizeClass at the app shell or route level.
Window width is usually the primary size-class signal because vertical scrolling is common.
Derive simple layout state such as onePane, twoPane, or showSupportingPane and pass it down explicitly.
Keep reusable child composables flexible; do not let every leaf query global window state.
Use panes for large-window layouts: list pane, detail pane, and optional supporting or extra panes.
NavigableListDetailPaneScaffold combines list-detail layout, pane navigation, and predictive-back-aware transitions.
Test compact, medium, expanded, split-screen, desktop-window, foldable posture, and large font/display scale cases.
gotchas_html:
• Using physical screen size. LocalConfiguration.current.screenWidthDp or display metrics can be wrong for split screen, desktop windows, and external displays. Use app-window metrics and window size classes.
  • Scattering breakpoint checks. When every row/card decides its own breakpoint, behavior drifts. Centralize screen policy and pass down state.
  • Hardcoding tablet widths. A fixed if (width > 600) rule misses posture, resizable windows, and future large-window classes. Prefer Material window size classes or pane scaffolds.
  • Making leaf components adaptive shells. A reusable item row should not know whether the app is on a foldable. It should accept constraints, modifier, and content state.
  • Ignoring back behavior in multi-pane UI. Back can mean return to list, previous selected detail, or exit depending on pane state. Pick and test the behavior intentionally.
  • Only testing portrait phone. Adaptive bugs appear during resizing, split screen, rotation, large font scale, and fold posture changes.

playground.intro: Treat the Scaffold controls as adaptive shell decisions. A compact window shows one pane with top navigation; wider windows can keep navigation visible, add a second pane, and still let inner rows stay reusable.

title: Recipe: Profile Row
summary: Combine Row alignment, weight, spacing, clip, and Text styles into the canonical list-item layout: a circular avatar, a name + subtitle column, and a trailing control pushed to the edge.
explanation_html:
The profile row is the most common composition you will ever build: an avatar on the left, a name and subtitle in the middle, and a control on the right. It is a contacts entry, a settings toggle, a chat preview, a playlist track. It is worth learning as one idiomatic pattern because it ties together five separate Compose concepts — Row, cross-axis alignment, the weight modifier, Spacer-based spacing, and clip — into a layout you will reach for constantly.

The skeleton is a single Row(verticalAlignment = Alignment.CenterVertically). The verticalAlignment parameter is set once on the Row and controls how every child sits on the cross (vertical) axis. CenterVertically is what makes the small avatar, the two-line text block, and the trailing icon all share a common centerline even though they have different heights — without it, children default to Alignment.Top and the avatar would cling to the top edge while the text drops below it.

The avatar is a fixed-size box clipped to a shape: Modifier.size(40.dp).clip(CircleShape). The clip modifier masks anything drawn after it — a background color, or an Image with contentScale = ContentScale.Crop — to that shape. Order matters: size then clip then background, because modifiers apply top-down and you want the clip to constrain the fill. Swapping CircleShape for RoundedCornerShape(12.dp) turns the same code into a rounded-square avatar.

The middle Column holds two Texts using the type scale: the name uses MaterialTheme.typography.titleMedium and the subtitle uses bodyMedium with a muted color — MaterialTheme.colorScheme.onSurfaceVariant. Using the theme's color role rather than a hardcoded grey is what makes the row adapt automatically to light and dark themes.

The final trick is Modifier.weight(1f). Applying it to the Column (or to a Spacer between the text and the control) makes that child expand to fill all leftover horizontal space, which shoves the trailing Icon or Switch hard against the end of the row. This is the standard "push to the end" technique in Compose — there is no justify-content: space-between; you express it with weight. Add even spacing between the avatar and text with a Spacer(Modifier.width(12.dp)) or by setting horizontalArrangement = Arrangement.spacedBy(12.dp) on the Row.
key_points: verticalAlignment = Alignment.CenterVertically is set once on the Row and aligns all children on the cross axis; the default is Alignment.Top.
Modifier.weight(1f) on the middle Column (or a Spacer) consumes all leftover horizontal space and pushes the trailing control to the end — Compose has no space-between.
Modifier order is meaningful: size → clip → background, so the clip mask constrains the fill; clip(CircleShape) yields a circle, clip(RoundedCornerShape(12.dp)) a rounded square.
For real images use Image(..., contentScale = ContentScale.Crop) inside the clipped Box so the bitmap fills the shape without distortion.
Use the type scale: titleMedium for the name, bodyMedium for the subtitle — don't hardcode font sizes.
Mute the subtitle with MaterialTheme.colorScheme.onSurfaceVariant instead of a fixed grey so it adapts to light/dark themes.
Space children with Spacer(Modifier.width(12.dp)) or horizontalArrangement = Arrangement.spacedBy(12.dp) on the Row.
weight defaults to fill = true, so a weighted child is forced to its allotted width even when its content is narrower.
gotchas_html:
• Forgetting verticalAlignment. Children default to Alignment.Top, so the avatar pins to the top and the text and icon look misaligned. Set verticalAlignment = Alignment.CenterVertically on the Row.
  • Clipping before sizing, or before the background. Modifier.background(...).clip(CircleShape) clips nothing visible because the background already painted. The correct order is size().clip(shape).background().
  • Reaching for fillMaxWidth() on the Column to push the icon over. Inside a Row that does nothing useful (or throws an unbounded-width error) — the tool you want is Modifier.weight(1f).
  • Putting weight on the avatar or the icon. Weight stretches whatever it's on; you almost always want it on the flexible text Column, leaving the avatar and control at their intrinsic sizes.
  • Hardcoding a grey subtitle. Color.Gray looks wrong in dark mode. Use onSurfaceVariant from the color scheme.
  • Letting a long name shove the icon off-screen. Add maxLines = 1, overflow = TextOverflow.Ellipsis to the name Text so it truncates instead of pushing the trailing control out of bounds.

playground.intro: Build the canonical list-item layout. Toggle the avatar shape, the subtitle, and the trailing control, and change how children line up on the cross axis to see why CenterVertically is the right default.

title: State saving: rememberSaveable, ViewModel & process death
summary: Compose state has a lifespan. Use remember for recomposition-only memory, rememberSaveable for small UI element state that should survive configuration changes and process recreation, and a ViewModel or state holder for screen state and business logic.
explanation_html:
remember is the smallest state tool: it keeps an object while the call remains in the composition, so it survives recomposition but not activity recreation. Rotate the device, change system language, or let the process die, and plain remember starts over. That is perfect for purely transient state such as an expanded menu that does not need to come back after recreation.

rememberSaveable is for small UI element state that should survive configuration changes and system-initiated process recreation. It stores values through Android's saved instance state mechanism, so the value must be saveable in a Bundle or converted through a Saver. Text input, selected tab index, dialog visibility, and scroll position are good candidates. A large loaded feed, database result, bitmap, or business object graph is not.

Screen-level state belongs in a state holder, often a ViewModel. The ViewModel survives configuration changes and coordinates business logic, repositories, validation, and one screen's source of truth. It should expose immutable UI state and receive events. It is not a replacement for persistence after process death; when process death matters, save only the minimal restoration key in SavedStateHandle and rebuild the larger state from your data layer.

The practical decision is: recomposition-only value? remember. Small value the user would expect to keep after rotation or process recreation? rememberSaveable. State produced by app data or business rules? ViewModel/state holder, with durable data in storage and only minimal UI element state in saved state.
key_points: remember survives recomposition while the composable remains in the composition; it does not survive activity recreation.
rememberSaveable survives configuration changes and process recreation by saving through Android saved instance state.
rememberSaveable is for small UI element state such as text, selected tab, expanded state, or scroll position.
Only save Bundle-friendly values directly; use a Saver for custom lightweight types.
Do not put large screen state, loaded collections, bitmaps, or business objects into saved instance state.
Use a ViewModel or state holder for screen UI state and business logic.
SavedStateHandle should hold minimal restoration keys or simple UI element state, not complex screen data.
After process recreation, rebuild screen UI state from the data layer plus saved keys.
gotchas_html:
• Using remember for text input. It works until rotation, then the input disappears. Use rememberSaveable for small user-entered text.
  • Saving too much. Saved instance state has size limits and serialization costs. Save IDs and simple UI state; reload large data from repositories.
  • Assuming ViewModel survives process death. It survives configuration changes, not a killed process. Use persistent storage and SavedStateHandle for restoration keys.
  • Passing a ViewModel into every composable. Keep leaf UI stateless and previewable: pass state and callbacks down from a screen-level route.
  • Trying to save non-saveable custom classes directly. Convert them with a Saver, or save only primitive fields.

playground.intro: Toggle TextField state and singleLine to model a small UI element that should usually be rememberSaveable, then contrast that with the larger results list that belongs in a state holder.

title: Effects & lifecycle: LaunchedEffect, DisposableEffect and derivedStateOf
summary: Composable bodies must stay side-effect free. Use effect APIs to run work after successful composition, tie coroutines and listeners to keys, clean up subscriptions, and derive cheap state without creating recomposition loops.
explanation_html:
A composable body can be run, skipped, restarted, or abandoned. That means it must describe UI, not start work directly. Network calls, timers, navigation side effects, analytics events, subscriptions, and coroutine launches belong in a controlled effect API so Compose can start and cancel them with the composable lifecycle.

LaunchedEffect(keys) starts a coroutine after the composition commits. If a key changes, Compose cancels the old coroutine and starts a new one. Use it for one-shot loads, snackbar events, scroll commands, and collecting flows when the work is scoped to this composable. Use stable, intentional keys: LaunchedEffect(Unit) means once for this call entering composition; LaunchedEffect(userId) means restart when the user changes.

DisposableEffect(keys) is for work that must be cleaned up: listeners, observers, callbacks registered with a platform API. It requires an onDispose block. SideEffect runs after every successful composition and is useful for publishing Compose state to non-Compose objects. rememberUpdatedState keeps the newest lambda or value visible to a long-running effect without restarting the effect.

derivedStateOf is for derived values that change less often than their inputs, especially when a high-frequency state such as scroll position drives a threshold like show/hide FAB. It is not a general replacement for plain Kotlin expressions; use it when it reduces invalidations.
key_points: Do not launch coroutines, register listeners, navigate, or mutate external state directly in a composable body.
LaunchedEffect starts a coroutine after successful composition and cancels/restarts it when its keys change.
DisposableEffect ties registration and cleanup to composition; every DisposableEffect needs onDispose.
SideEffect publishes values after every successful composition.
rememberUpdatedState lets a long-running effect see the newest lambda or value without restarting.
derivedStateOf is useful when many input changes collapse into fewer output changes.
Bad keys either restart effects too often or keep stale work alive too long.
Event callbacks are safe places for user-triggered side effects such as button-click navigation.
gotchas_html:
• Calling suspend functions in the body. A composable is not a coroutine scope. Use LaunchedEffect or a click callback.
  • Using random or changing keys. A changing key restarts the effect constantly. Key effects to the identity of the work.
  • Capturing stale lambdas. Long-running effects can call an old callback unless you wrap it with rememberUpdatedState.
  • Forgetting cleanup. Registering an observer without DisposableEffect leaks work after the composable leaves the screen.
  • Overusing derivedStateOf. If the derived value changes every time the input changes, a plain expression is clearer.

playground.intro: Treat the sliders as effect keys: if a key changes, Compose cancels the old effect and starts the new one. Use the color and size controls to model visible state, but keep the side effect itself outside the body.

title: Performance & stability: skippability, stable params and phase-aware reads
summary: Compose is fast when it can skip work. Stable immutable parameters, focused state reads, lazy lists, keys, and phase-aware modifiers keep recomposition, layout, and drawing scoped to the work that actually changed.
explanation_html:
Compose performance is mostly about avoiding unnecessary work. The runtime tracks state reads and restarts only the affected scopes, but it can skip even more when a composable's parameters are stable and unchanged. Immutable data classes with val properties and persistent collections are easier for Compose to reason about than mutable objects that can change behind its back.

Stability is Compose's confidence that a value either cannot change or will notify Compose when it changes. Stable parameters help composables become skippable; unstable parameters can force recomposition because the compiler cannot know whether the value changed. Diagnose before fixing: use Layout Inspector, compiler reports, tracing, and targeted measurement rather than guessing.

Where you read state also matters. A state read in composition can trigger recomposition. A read in layout can restart layout. A read in drawing can skip composition and layout and redraw only. For high-frequency visual changes such as animated colors or canvas values, prefer lambda/draw modifiers like drawBehind when appropriate so the change happens in the draw phase.

Lists need stable item keys when item identity matters. Without keys, inserting at the top can make Compose treat many rows as changed. With items(messages, key = { it.id }), state and animations follow the item identity instead of the index.
key_points: Stable immutable parameters make it easier for Compose to skip unchanged composables.
Mutable objects passed as params can look unstable because Compose cannot know whether internal fields changed.
Use compiler stability reports, Layout Inspector, and tracing before optimizing.
Read state in the narrowest phase that needs it: composition, layout, or drawing.
High-frequency draw-only changes often belong in drawBehind/drawWithContent rather than recomposition.
Lazy lists should use stable keys when items can move, insert, or delete.
Do not optimize by hoisting every value globally; keep state close to where it is consumed.
Performance fixes should preserve correctness, accessibility, and readability.
gotchas_html:
• Mutating a list in place. Compose may not observe the mutation and item identity becomes unclear. Prefer immutable lists exposed as new values.
  • Using index as identity. Insertions reorder state. Use stable IDs for lazy list keys.
  • Reading scroll state at the top of a screen. That can recompose too much. Use derivedStateOf or read in the phase that needs it.
  • Blindly adding @Stable. An incorrect stability promise can create stale UI. Diagnose first.
  • Optimizing samples before measuring. Compose is already scoped; measure real bottlenecks before adding complexity.

playground.intro: Use child count and gap to model lazy-list churn. Stable keys preserve row identity as items move; without keys, state follows positions and recomposition work grows.

title: Performance measurement: Macrobenchmark, Baseline Profiles, JankStats and compiler reports
summary: Production Compose performance is a measurement workflow. Use Macrobenchmark on release-like builds and physical devices, generate Baseline Profiles for startup and critical user journeys, add JankStats state for runtime frame context, and turn on Compose compiler reports only when stability evidence matters.
explanation_html:
Compose performance work should start with evidence, not folklore. A debug build, an emulator, or a recomposition counter can point you in a direction, but they do not prove user-facing performance. The production workflow is: choose a critical user journey, measure it with a release-like build, read the trace and metrics, change one thing, and re-measure. For Compose screens, those journeys are usually cold startup, opening a destination, scrolling a lazy list, animating content, loading a feed, or moving through a navigation path.

Macrobenchmark is the right tool for whole-app measurements. It runs outside the target app and can measure startup with StartupTimingMetric, runtime frames with FrameTimingMetric, and custom trace sections with TraceSectionMetric. It also controls pre-measurement compilation through CompilationMode, so you can compare no compilation, partial compilation with Baseline Profiles, warmup-based partial compilation, and full compilation. Run these benchmarks on physical devices; emulator timing is not reliable enough for performance decisions.

Baseline Profiles are not tests. They are generated ART profile rules that tell Android which code paths to ahead-of-time compile after install or update. Compose apps benefit because the UI toolkit and app code otherwise rely more heavily on interpretation and JIT during first use. A strong profile covers startup plus critical user journeys: scrolling lists, opening important screens, navigating between destinations, running common animations, and interacting with controls. Keep startup-related collection inside rules with includeInStartupProfile = true, and keep non-startup journeys out of the Startup Profile when that distinction matters.

JankStats complements benchmarks in runtime or dogfood builds. Macrobenchmark tells you whether a measured journey regressed; JankStats tells you where janky frames occur with app state attached. In Compose, remember a PerformanceMetricsState.Holder from LocalView.current, then update state from effects such as snapshotFlow { listState.isScrollInProgress }. Keep frame callbacks tiny, copy data you need, and pause tracking when the Activity is paused.

Compose compiler reports are a diagnostic, not a goal. They show inferred stability, restartability, and skippability for composables and classes, and should be generated from release builds when a measured problem suggests unnecessary recomposition or missed skipping. Do not try to make every composable skippable by adding annotations blindly. First prove which journey is slow; then use Layout Inspector, traces, compiler reports, and code review to explain why.
key_points: Measure real user journeys before optimizing Compose performance.
Run Macrobenchmark on physical devices and release-like target builds, not debug builds or emulator timing.
Use StartupTimingMetric for app startup, FrameTimingMetric for scrolling and animation, and TraceSectionMetric for custom traced work.
Use CompilationMode to compare no compilation, Baseline Profile partial compilation, warmup partial compilation, and full compilation.
Generate Baseline Profiles from startup and critical user journeys, not just the launcher Activity.
Use includeInStartupProfile for startup profile collection only when the journey is part of startup.
Benchmark Baseline Profile impact by comparing CompilationMode.None with CompilationMode.Partial using BaselineProfileMode.Require.
Use JankStats to attach UI state to janky frames in runtime observation, and update state from effects instead of composition.
Use Compose compiler reports from release builds to inspect stability, restartability, and skippability after a measured problem exists.
Treat performance work as an experiment: one change, repeatable benchmark, trace evidence, and a recorded result.
gotchas_html:
• Benchmarking debug builds. Debuggable apps and debug Compose settings do not represent users. Macrobenchmark expects a non-debuggable/profileable target and reports configuration errors for bad setups.
  • Trusting emulator timings. Emulators share host resources and can produce misleading results. Use physical devices for benchmark evidence.
  • Generating a launch-only Baseline Profile. Startup matters, but Compose jank often appears in scrolling, navigation, and animation paths. Include critical user journeys.
  • Putting every profile rule in the Startup Profile. Startup Profiles are the startup subset. Keep non-startup journeys out of includeInStartupProfile unless they truly affect startup.
  • Comparing benchmark runs with different compilation states accidentally. Name and control CompilationMode explicitly when proving Baseline Profile benefit.
  • Doing heavy work in JankStats callbacks. Frame callbacks must return quickly. Copy minimal data and aggregate later.
  • Using compiler reports as a scorecard. Skippability is useful only when it explains a measured issue. Blind @Stable or @Immutable can create stale UI or maintenance debt.
  • Optimizing without trace labels. Add meaningful trace sections around expensive work so Macrobenchmark and Perfetto can show where time went.

playground.intro: Switch between measurement tools and adjust the simulated startup/frame numbers. The generated Kotlin changes between Macrobenchmark, Baseline Profile generation, JankStats state reporting, and compiler report setup.

title: Previews & tooling: @Preview, multipreview, Layout Inspector and lint
summary: Compose tooling turns UI work into a tight feedback loop. Use stateless themed previews for sample states, multipreview templates for screen sizes, font scales, light/dark and dynamic color, PreviewParameterProvider for data variants, then use UI Check, Layout Inspector, Animation Preview, tracing, and Compose lint to catch issues previews alone cannot prove.
explanation_html:
@Preview is Android Studio's fastest Compose feedback loop. A preview renders a composable in the design surface without launching the full app, which makes it ideal for component states, screen content states, empty/error/loading variants, long text, different locales, light and dark theme, large font scale, and representative device sizes. The preview should usually target the stateless content composable: state in, events out, wrapped in the app theme with sample data and no-op callbacks.

Multipreview turns one preview function into a matrix. Built-in templates such as @PreviewScreenSizes, @PreviewFontScales, @PreviewLightDark, and @PreviewDynamicColors cover common variants with less boilerplate. Custom multipreview annotations let a team encode its own standards: compact/expanded width, important locales, accessibility font scale, dark mode, and branded backgrounds. PreviewParameterProvider is the data side of the same idea: one preview function can render several sample states from a provider.

Previews run in Layoutlib, not in your production app process. That keeps them lightweight, but it also means no network access, no file access, and limited availability for some Context APIs. A route composable that calls viewModel(), hiltViewModel(), repositories, navigation, or real platform services is the wrong preview target. Keep that wiring at the route boundary and preview the content function with explicit parameters.

Android Studio tooling fills the gaps. Compose UI Check runs from Preview and audits accessibility and adaptive issues such as contrast, stretched content, and size problems. Layout Inspector inspects a running app, shows the Compose hierarchy, semantics, recomposition counts, and skipped compositions. Animation Preview can scrub, pause, slow down, coordinate, and inspect supported Compose animations when they are exposed through a preview. Composition tracing adds composable names to system traces for performance investigation, and Compose lint catches correctness issues before release.

The workflow is layered: preview first for fast design and state coverage, UI Check for accessibility/adaptive scanning, Compose UI tests for behavior, Layout Inspector and tracing for runtime truth, and lint for static correctness. A preview is not a test and not a benchmark. It is the cheapest way to make many UI states visible before they escape into code review.
key_points: @Preview should render small, deterministic, stateless UI surfaces with sample data and no-op callbacks.
Wrap previews in the same app theme or design-system theme used in production.
Preview content composables, not route composables that obtain ViewModels, repositories, NavController, or Hilt dependencies.
Use multipreview templates such as PreviewScreenSizes, PreviewFontScales, PreviewLightDark, and PreviewDynamicColors to cover common variants.
Use custom multipreview annotations when the team has a standard matrix of locales, font scales, devices, or themes.
Use PreviewParameterProvider to feed several realistic UI states into one preview function.
Remember Layoutlib limits: previews are lightweight, but they do not have network access, file access, or every Context API.
Use Compose UI Check from previews to find accessibility and adaptive layout problems early.
Use Layout Inspector and composition tracing for runtime evidence about hierarchy, semantics, recomposition, skipped composition, and performance.
Use Animation Preview for supported animation APIs, and keep Compose lint enabled for static correctness checks.
gotchas_html:
• Previewing a route that creates a ViewModel. Layoutlib cannot build your Hilt graph, repositories, navigation, or real platform services. Preview the content function with sample state instead.
  • Un-themed previews. A bare preview can hide typography, color, shape, dynamic color, and dark-theme problems. Wrap content in the app theme.
  • Only previewing the happy path. Empty, loading, error, long text, right-to-left, dark mode, and large font scale are where real UI breaks.
  • Doing work in previews. Network calls, file reads, and expensive startup work are unreliable or unavailable in Layoutlib and make previews slow.
  • Treating previews as tests. Previews catch visual states, but they do not click, type, assert behavior, or synchronize with app logic. Pair them with Compose UI tests.
  • Ignoring UI Check and Layout Inspector. The preview image is only the first signal; use the tooling modes to inspect semantics, recompositions, adaptive issues, and contrast.
  • Suppressing Compose lint instead of fixing it. Lint often points to real Compose correctness issues. Suppress only with a documented reason.

playground.intro: Use this row as a preview matrix stand-in: toggle text, trailing state, and decorative content, then imagine rendering the same component across light/dark, font scale, screen sizes, and sample data states.

title: Accessibility, semantics & Compose tests
summary: Compose tests and accessibility both depend on the semantics tree. Build meaningful semantics, merge or clear nodes deliberately, provide labels and roles, and test behavior through semantic matchers rather than view implementation details.
explanation_html:
Compose builds a semantics tree alongside the UI tree. Accessibility services use it to describe and operate the screen, and Compose UI tests use it to find nodes and assert behavior. That shared model is why good accessibility makes tests better too.

Material components provide many semantics defaults, but custom UI needs deliberate labels, roles, state descriptions, actions, and traversal behavior. Decorative icons should use contentDescription = null; meaningful icons need a clear description. A row that acts like one button should usually merge descendant semantics so TalkBack focuses the whole row, not every tiny child.

Testing should target user-observable behavior. Instead of finding implementation-specific boxes, use onNodeWithText, onNodeWithContentDescription, semantic roles, test tags for otherwise invisible affordances, and assertions such as assertIsDisplayed or assertIsOn. Tests should click, type, scroll, and assert the UI state the user sees.

Accessibility quality also includes scale. Text should use sp, layouts should survive large font/display scale, touch targets should remain large enough, and focus order should match visual order. Compose gives you tools like semantics, clearAndSetSemantics, selectableGroup, and UI test APIs to make that visible and verifiable.
key_points: The semantics tree powers both accessibility services and Compose UI tests.
Material components provide defaults, but custom composables need deliberate labels, roles, state descriptions, and actions.
Use null contentDescription for decorative icons and meaningful descriptions for actionable icons.
Use mergeDescendants when a row/card should be one accessible target.
Use clearAndSetSemantics only when replacing noisy child semantics with a better single description.
Compose tests should assert user-observable semantics, not internal layout details.
Use selectableGroup for radio groups and similar mutually exclusive choices.
Test large font/display scale and touch target sizing, not only the default emulator settings.
gotchas_html:
• Every icon gets a label. Decorative icons with labels create noisy repeated announcements. Use contentDescription = null when the adjacent text already explains it.
  • Clickable Row with no role. A custom clickable surface may be announced vaguely unless semantics describe its role/action.
  • Overusing test tags. Prefer text/content descriptions when they represent real user-visible affordances. Use tags for invisible test hooks.
  • Testing layout instead of behavior. A test that depends on internal Column/Row structure breaks during harmless refactors.
  • Ignoring font scale. Accessibility failures often appear only at larger text and display sizes.

playground.intro: Toggle the trailing control and subtitle to model what should be merged into one semantic row. A user should hear one useful target, not a pile of decorative implementation details.

title: Compose UI testing: rules, semantics and synchronization
summary: Compose UI tests are built around the semantics tree, ComposeTestRule, deterministic actions/assertions, and a virtual main clock. Good tests host the smallest useful surface, find meaningful nodes, perform user actions, and assert behavior without sleeping or coupling to Row/Column internals.
explanation_html:
Compose UI testing is different from View testing because only some composables emit nodes. The reliable contract is the semantics tree: text, content descriptions, roles, actions, state descriptions, selected/checked state, progress, and explicit test tags. A test should describe what the user can perceive or do, not the implementation containers used to lay out the screen.

ComposeTestRule is the center of most tests. It hosts content with setContent, finds one or many semantic nodes with APIs such as onNode, onAllNodes, onNodeWithText, and onNodeWithContentDescription, then runs assertions and actions such as assertIsDisplayed, assertIsEnabled, performClick, performTextInput, and scroll actions. Prefer visible text, labels, roles, and state matchers; use Modifier.testTag when a stable test hook is needed for something not otherwise exposed to users.

Compose tests synchronize by default. Before actions and assertions, the rule waits until the Compose UI is idle and advances Compose time through a virtual clock. That is why arbitrary Thread.sleep calls make tests slower and flakier. For animations and gesture timing, turn off mainClock.autoAdvance only when you intentionally need frame-by-frame control, then advance time with advanceTimeByFrame or advanceTimeBy.

Use the right scope. Test reusable composables in isolation with fake state and callbacks; test route or screen behavior when navigation, ViewModel collection, resources, or app wiring matters. Debug failing selectors by printing the semantics tree with onRoot().printToLog(). When upgrading, check the current testing v2 migration notes so imports and dispatcher behavior match the Compose version in the app.
key_points: Compose UI tests interact with semantic nodes, not every composable function call.
Use ComposeTestRule to host content, find nodes, perform actions, make assertions, and synchronize with the Compose UI.
Prefer user-facing selectors: text, content descriptions, roles, state, click actions, and semantic matchers.
Use Modifier.testTag for stable test hooks only when visible or accessibility selectors are not the right contract.
Assert behavior and state changes, not Row/Column/Box structure, child indexes, padding, or exact pixel positions.
Compose tests synchronize by default before actions and assertions; do not add arbitrary sleeps.
Use mainClock.autoAdvance = false plus advanceTimeBy/advanceTimeByFrame for deterministic animation or gesture timing.
Test small composables in isolation with fake state, but add screen-level tests for routing, resources, and integration behavior.
Use onRoot().printToLog() and useUnmergedTree only when debugging merged semantics hides the node you need.
Check the current testing v2 migration guide when updating Compose test APIs.
gotchas_html:
• Testing implementation containers. A test that walks child indexes or asserts exact Row/Column sizes breaks when the layout is refactored without changing behavior.
  • Sleeping for async or animation. Thread.sleep makes tests slow and flaky. Use the Compose test rule's synchronization, waitUntil, idling resources, or mainClock.
  • Using test tags as product semantics. Tags are for tests. They do not replace content descriptions, roles, state descriptions, or accessible labels.
  • Forgetting merged semantics. A custom row may merge descendants into one node, so a child may not be visible in the default tree. Inspect the tree before forcing useUnmergedTree.
  • Testing previews instead of behavior. Previews catch visual states, but UI tests must still click, type, scroll, and assert user-observable outcomes.
  • Asserting before state is driven. If the test mutates state outside synchronized APIs, call a synchronized action/assertion or waitForIdle() before checking the result.
  • Putting real ViewModels in component tests. Reusable composables should be tested with plain state and lambdas; ViewModel tests belong at the route or integration boundary.

playground.intro: Toggle row content and state to think like a Compose test. The reliable selector is the semantic contract the user can perceive: text, role, state, action, or a deliberate test tag.

title: Animation & motion: choose the right API
summary: Compose animation APIs map to the kind of change you are expressing: animate a single value with animate*AsState, multiple coordinated values with updateTransition, content changes with AnimatedContent, visibility with AnimatedVisibility, and size changes with animateContentSize.
explanation_html:
Compose animation is state-driven: describe the target state, and the animation system moves from the current value to the target. You do not manually mutate frames. The most important skill is picking the smallest API that matches the change.

For one value, use animateDpAsState, animateColorAsState, animateFloatAsState, and related helpers. They are ideal for a button elevation, card color, alpha, corner radius, or offset that follows a Boolean or enum target. For several values that must coordinate off the same state, use updateTransition.

When content itself changes, use composable-level APIs. AnimatedVisibility animates enter and exit. AnimatedContent animates between target content states. Crossfade fades between content. Modifier.animateContentSize() animates size changes caused by content expansion or collapse.

Use explicit AnimationSpec values when motion needs a product feel: spring for physical settling, tween for fixed-duration easing, and keyframes for staged motion. Keep animation labels meaningful so Android Studio tooling can inspect them.
key_points: Animations are driven by target state, not manual frame mutation.
Use animate*AsState for a single animated value.
Use updateTransition when multiple values must animate from the same state.
Use AnimatedVisibility for enter/exit and AnimatedContent for content changes.
Use animateContentSize when layout size changes because content expands or collapses.
Use spring, tween, or keyframes through AnimationSpec when the default feel is not right.
Provide labels for animation tooling and debugging.
Respect reduced-motion expectations by avoiding unnecessary decorative motion.
gotchas_html:
• Starting animation loops in the body. Animation should be derived from state or run in an effect, not manually started on every recomposition.
  • Using AnimatedVisibility to swap unrelated content. Use AnimatedContent when the content state changes from one thing to another.
  • Animating layout with offset only. offset moves drawing/placement but may not affect sibling layout. Use the right layout animation when surrounding content should respond.
  • Forgetting labels. Anonymous animations are harder to inspect in tooling.
  • Animating everything. Motion should clarify state change; too much motion makes UI feel slower and less accessible.

playground.intro: Change fill, corner radius, and color as stand-ins for target animation state. In real Compose, animate*AsState would interpolate between these target values when state changes.
